author: BUPTlhuanyu

title: How to change asset type for inline nested loaders?
body:
I'm having the exact same issue as @rb-mwindh was having in [this issue](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12531).

To recap, Angular generates a highly opinionated webpack config that they make difficult to change, so when doing anything that Angular doesn't expect you to be doing, there are problems. I'm trying to apply some css via style-loader and css-loader, but Angular sets the asset type of all .css imports as `asset/resource`. [The solution](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12531#issuecomment-947760871) posted in the issue above uses an internal `!=!` syntax like this to change the resource type so that the loader output is evaluated: 

```
import styles from 'style.css.webpack[javascript/auto]!=!!!style-loader?injectType=lazyStyleTag!css-loader!some-npm-module/style.css`;
```

This works great for the style-loader, but the output of the nested css-loader is still being treated as an `asset/resource`. Is it possible to apply the same trick to change the type for css-loader too? My attempts at guessing at the syntax have so far failed. Thanks!

title: Dynamic import returns undefined when using variable query string
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
Dynamic import returns undefined when using variable query string, but when using normal string it works as expected

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
bring your own svg (or use this https://freesvg.org/nicubunu-potato) and use the following code:

```
import React, { useState, useEffect, useRef } from 'react';

function useDynamicSVGImport(name) {
  const ImportedIconRef = useRef();
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);
  const [error, setError] = useState();

  useEffect(() => {
    setLoading(true);
    const importIcon = async () => {
      try {
        ImportedIconRef.current = (
          await import( '../icons/' + name + '.svg')
        ).ReactComponent;
      } catch (err) {
        setError(err);
        throw err;
      } finally {
        setLoading(false);
      }
    };
    importIcon();
  }, [name]);

  return { error, loading, SvgIcon: ImportedIconRef.current };
}

const Icon = ({ name, ...rest }) => {
  const { loading, SvgIcon } = useDynamicSVGImport(name);

  if (loading) {
    return null;
  }

  if (SvgIcon) {
    return <SvgIcon {...rest} />;
  }
  return null;
};


<Icon name="icon" />
```

replacing import with `await import( '../icons/icon.svg')` works

**What is the expected behavior?**
it should return the svg


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.64.4 (using CRA)
Node.js version: 14.5.0
Operating System: Windows
Additional tools: react scripts 5.0.0

body:

**What is the expected behavior?**

I can't get env parameter when i using webpack config as function

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

 I'm building a web server with using webpack and webpack-hot-middleware.

When i put `webpack-hot-middleware/client` to every entry like this, my production build bundle file includes `webpack-hot-middleware` package codes

So, I want to put `webpack-hot-middleware/client` to entry array when **only development enviroment** 

Like this

[Referring to this pages to seperate env files](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/)

**webpack.config.js**
```javascript
module.exports = (env, argv) => {
  const config = {};
  const mode = argv.mode || 'development';

  const entry = {
        test: ['./src/script/test.ts'],
    };

  if(mode === 'development'){
    Object.keys(entry).forEach((key) => (entry[key].push('webpack-hot-middleware/client'))
  }

  ...
}
```

**app.js (server.js)**
```javascript
const webpackHotMiddleware = require('webpack-hot-middleware');
const webpackDevMiddleware = require('webpack-dev-middleware');
const webpack = require('webpack');

const webpackConfig = require('./webpack.config');
const compiler = webpack(webpackConfig());

if (node_env === 'local') {
    app.use(webpackDevMiddleware(compiler));
    app.use(webpackHotMiddleware(compiler, { path: '/__webpack_hmr' }));
}
```

**package.json**
```json
"scripts": {
  "start": "webpack --mode=development & npm run app.js"
}
```

Todo pass env, argv parameter to webpack.config.json, i change webpack.config.json Object to function
So i have to pass config like this (using function)
```javascript
const compiler = webpack(webpackConfig());
```

But, I Can't pass parameter to webpack config (Because no parameter in webpackConfig function) and `env`, `argv` parameters are undefined

So, as a temporary measure, Optional Chaining has been used.

```javascript
const mode = argv?.mode || 'development';
```

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

I want to be able to receive the env parameter values by using callback function.

Like this

```javascript
const compiler = webpack(((env, argv) => webpackConfig(env, argv))());
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

title: understanding & implementing tree shaking properly
body:
My organization has a component library that we package up with [`tsdx`](https://tsdx.io/), publish to our org's GitHub package registry, and consume in various applications. The library makes some use of icons from a package called `@icons/material`. Until recently, all icons were used by importing a default export, like:

`import SomeIcon from '@icons/material/SomeIcon';`

But in a recent update, one file imported two icons as named exports...

`import { Icon1, Icon2 } from '@icons/material';`

...and when I updated the library into one of the consuming apps (which is bundled up with a pretty straightforward, manually-configured webpack 5 setup), I noticed a huge increase in the bundle size. The `webpack-bundle-analyzer` plugin showed me the issue pretty quickly: every icon from `@icons/material` was now getting pulled into the bundle.

From here, I thought I just needed to learn to properly use webpack tree shaking (https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/) in the consuming app, but so far I'm not making progress. After reading the docs a couple of times it seemed to me that I needed `"sideEffects": false` in the `package.json` of either the library, or the consuming app, or maybe both, but none of those have worked for me.

I realize I could just change the format of those imports in the library, but I feel like that's just going to leave the issue waiting to recur in future updates, and there shouldn't be anything wrong with using that import syntax. I'd like to solve this at the consuming app's bundling stage, but I'm kind of stuck now — anyone have a suggestion for what to try next?

author: Vanillabacke

title: CacheInputFileSystem default cache time
body:
Basically, similar question as #15036. `CacheInputFileSystem` only caches for 1 minute. In longer builds, useful fs entries will be purged away, requiring a second identical fs call. One example that stands out is https://github.com/xz64/license-webpack-plugin, which does a bunch of fs calls very late in the compilation process to grab license information.

It seems 1min was chosen arbitrarily - what are y'alls thoughts on (1) raising it much higher, or (2) perhaps allowing setting the value via config w/ default

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/fbcc470bcf6a2fcf7494f6f720638d06a5166ea2/lib/node/NodeEnvironmentPlugin.js#L44

cc @alexander-akait

author: allaire

title: Native CSS support opportunities
body:
Hello webpack team.

I am one of the developers of a library named [Stylable](https://stylable.io/), it's a CSS pre-processor which involves a deep [integration ](https://github.com/wix/stylable/tree/master/packages/webpack-plugin) with `webpack` and `mini-css-extract-plugin`.
Currently part of our integration feels a bit hacky because some of the APIs we required are not documented and so we learned from reading the code. 
We saw the initial work around Native CSS support and we think this will be a great opportunity to simplify our integration and use official API's

Due to the complexity of our integration, we'd like to propose a few APIs we'll be needing from the upcoming built-in CSS integration:

* Add/inject additional `CssModule`s from our plugin
* Generate JS modules similar to `CSS modules` but with runtime functionality backed in, and having different export structure
* Exclude `.st.css` file extension from the built-in webpack CSS process or somehow use the built-in integrations as a post-process for asset handling and chunking 
* Optimize the JS modules and emitted CSS respectively before emitting bundles. (e.g. make classnames shorter and remove unused components CSS)

I hope we can start a discussion around adding these APIs/features so we'll be able to continue maintaining a clean and efficient integration to webpack.
Thanks in advance.

title: How to make webpack generate separate CSS and JS with the same name "index": "index.js" and "index.css" when there are multiple CSS outputs?
body:
Also asked at: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/70698775/how-to-make-webpack-generate-separate-css-and-js-with-the-same-name-index-in I was able to produce separate CSS and Js as mentioned at: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35322958/can-i-use-webpack-to-generate-css-and-js-separately/59237067#59237067 The working baseline code below produces files named: ``` dist/index.js dist/main.css dist/main.js dist/notmain.css dist/notmain.js ``` Note that undesired `main.js` and `notmain.js` dummy files [which no ones knows how to nicely fix](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/mini-css-extract-plugin/issues/151#issuecomment-749166647), but it doesn't matter to me in this case. My question is, how can I get Webpack to instead generate: ``` dist/index.css dist/index.js dist/notmain.css ``` i.e., rename `main.css` to `index.css`? ? Naively, what I wanted would be to just modify the working baseline to: ``` entry: { index: ['./index.js'], index: ['./index.scss'], }, ``` but then that wouldn't make any sense, since the `entry` dict now has two `index` keys, and the second one would erase the first. Also, if I had just a single CSS output, I could just hardcode: ``` new MiniCssExtractPlugin({ filename: 'index.css', }), ``` but since I have multiple ones, that won't cut it, I need that `[name]` placeholder to work somehow. I've seen at https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#output-filename that it documents that something like: ``` main: { import: './main.scss', filename: 'index.css', }, ``` could work, but if I try that it fails with: ``` ERROR in index.css index.css from Css Minimizer /home/ciro/bak/git/cirosantilli.github.io/webpack/tmp/index.css:1:10: Unknown word [index.css:1,10] ``` A similar question about this error message can be seen at: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50261548/webpack-fails-module-build-failed-unknown-word-with-webpack-config-js-file What happens is that for some reason `dist/index.css` contains a JavaScript file, and the then CSS minifier fails to parse it. An analogous change for `index.js` did work for JavaScript though, it's something specific to this CSS setup. **Working baseline code** index.html ``` Sass import

Hello

``` index.js ``` document.getElementsByTagName('body')[0].innerHTML += '

js works

' ``` main.scss ``` body { background-color: red; } ``` notmain.scss ``` body { color: blue; } ``` webpack.config.js ``` const path = require('path'); const CssMinimizerPlugin = require('css-minimizer-webpack-plugin'); const MiniCssExtractPlugin = require('mini-css-extract-plugin'); const nodeModulesPath = path.resolve(__dirname, 'node_modules'); module.exports = { entry: { index: ['./index.js'], main: ['./main.scss'], notmain: ['./notmain.scss'], }, mode: 'none', module: { rules: [ { test: /\.(scss|css)$/, use: [ MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader, 'css-loader', 'sass-loader', ], }, ], }, plugins: [ new MiniCssExtractPlugin({ filename: '[name].css', }), ], optimization: { minimizer: [ new CssMinimizerPlugin(), ], minimize: true, }, output: { filename: '[name].js', }, }; ``` package.json ``` { "name": "webpack-cheat", "version": "1.0.0", "private": true, "scripts": { "build": "webpack", "sass": "rm -f dist/main.css && sass -I node_modules main.scss dist/main.css" }, "author": "Ciro Santilli", "license": "MIT", "devDependencies": { "css-loader": "5.2.4", "css-minimizer-webpack-plugin": "3.0.2", "mini-css-extract-plugin": "2.1.0", "normalize.css": "8.0.1", "sass": "1.32.11", "sass-loader": "11.0.1", "style-loader": "2.0.0", "webpack": "5.36.1", "webpack-cli": "4.6.0", "webpack-dev-server": "3.11.2" } } ``` Compile and run: ``` npm install npm run build xdg-open index.html ```

title: Why does the configuration schema change so often?
body:
This is a bit of a philosophical question I've had for a while with webpack. Why does the schema for the configuration file change so often between versions? The way I see this most is that old advice on StackOverflow is usually wrong, because some property will have changed name since it was posted, or the many different ways of specifying a single property have changed in backwards-incompatible ways (eg the many ways of specifying `use`, `loader`, `loaders`, etc).

This causes problems, especially for new users / ones not deeply familiar with webpack, in that most old examples about how to configure webpack don't work anymore — if you find two answers on StackOverflow that should combine to serve your needs, usually this won't work because they are answering for different versions of webpack, and probably both are out of date with the current version, and migrating them to the current syntax is very nonobvious (and some old default plugins may not even work anymore or be superseded by some new plugin).

This is the primary reason I find webpack so hard to work with when setting up something complex.

On the pragmatic end of this question: How can we mitigate this problem? Are there feasible ways to detect this kind of problem and report it in a way that users not very familiar with webpack can use to migrate parts of their configuration correctly?

title: Migrate project webpack 4 to 5 : ProvidePlugin issue ?
body:
Hi,

I am migrating a web project from webpack 4 to webpack 5.

In console, i have the following error :

`rivets is not defined`

In webpack 4, without ProvidePlugin, the development output is : 

`eval("(function () {\n  var $document,\n      Cart,\n      CartJS,\n      FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING,\n`

In webpack 4, when i add ProvidePlugin : 

```
new webpack.ProvidePlugin({
  $: 'jquery',
  jQuery: 'jquery',
  rivets: 'rivets',
});
```
Everything is working and development output is now with the wrapper  :

`eval("/* WEBPACK VAR INJECTION */(function(jQuery, rivets) {(function () {\n  var $document,\n      Cart,\n      CartJS,\n      FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING,\n`

But now in webpack 5, the output is always without the wrapper `(function(jQuery, rivets) {` (even with ProvidePlugin) :

`eval("(function() {\n  var $document, Cart, CartJS, FORMAT_MONEY_WARNING`

Am i missing something ? 

Thanks.
Best regards.

Hi everyone,

`css-loader` is depending on `postcss`, which is depending on a [vulnerable version](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-NANOID-2332193) of `nanoid`. It looks like the `postcss` maintainers have recently upgraded their dependency on `nanoid` but not published a new release yet: [postcss/postcss#3636eaf9b749c64c5dbfbaa56d45bc22b3169ff9](https://github.com/postcss/postcss/commit/3636eaf9b749c64c5dbfbaa56d45bc22b3169ff9)

I'm not a Javascript expert and I'm not sure what the best way forward is here but still wanted to make the webpack maintainers aware. Let me know if I can help in any way.

Thanks,
Lennart

title: Is it possible to maintain tree-shaking an object when accessing a non-existing property?
body:
Suppose we have a module like this:
```js
export default {
  foo: "",
  bar: ""
}
```
This is how the module is used:
```js
import values from "./module";
console.log(values.doesNotExist);
```
This will cause webpack to add the entire upper module into the bundle, probably to protect any potential black-magic side-effects that could make this property access work.
Is it possible to disable this fallback and keep tree-shaking enabled?

title: CompatibilityPlugin and Shared dependencies, MF sync
body:
Still not 100% if this should be considered a bug, so before create an issue ticket i want to confirm it

after debug for a few days i found something that i still thinking is wrong

in webpack when we import a library that its already a bundle in webpack, during the build process we call the plugin `CompatibilityPlugin` that basically transforms `__webpack_require__` to `__nested_webpack_require_${pattern.range[0]}__`

my concern is that shared dependencies that were loaded by the main bundle are under `__webpack_require__` and these should be exposed to the whole application if these are `singleton`

but testing the bundle compiled i can see things like this
![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 09-12-56](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151135245-3ed69456-c0aa-4124-ad21-5775a4c5ad41.png)
![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 09-14-08](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151135404-18432975-30ff-4446-aa23-44443359202a.png)



if i marked that `react` should be singleton, why is trying to load it from the nested webpack bundle instance `__nested_webpack_require_${pattern.range[0]}__` and this is why is loading the "duplicated react" instead the original from the bundle

i can understand that each nested dependency in the bundle are completely isolated, but for scenarios where dependencies are shared/singleton doesnt make sense

should this be considered a bug ?

i did some tests doing manual changes in the bundle related to this, and was able to make works react instance

![Screenshot from 2022-01-26 10-11-55](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151144545-4c353d5a-f976-4c9a-adab-6a481c45b33f.png)

from
`var versions = scope[name] = scope[name] || {};`
to
`var versions = scope[dependencyName] = __webpack_require__.S[name][dependencyName] || scope[dependencyName] || {};` (keep on mind that this is for the scenario where `__webpack_require__` is nested and we need access to the root)

doing just 1 change i was able to make it works

title: moduleFederationPlugin load remote project  which with single runtime occur error
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Suppose I have two projects A and B, both of which use a single runtime, and then when I reference B in project A, I find that an error will be reported during debugging:  **"`__webpack_require__ is not defined`"**

### Config of project `A`

```js
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require('webpack/lib').container;
const name = 'A';
module.exports = {
  devtool: false,
  devServer: {
     port: 8061
  },
  output: {
    chunkLoadingGlobal: `${name}Chunks`,
    publicPath: 'auto',
    filename: `${name}/[name].js`
  },
  optimization: {
      runtimeChunk: { name: 'runtime' }
  },
  plugins: [
     new ModuleFederationPlugin({
       name: name,
       filename: `${name}/container.js`,
       runtime: 'runtime',
       remotes: {
          B:  ['B@http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/runtime.js', 'B@http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/container.js'] 
      }
    })
  ]
};
```

### Config of project `B`

```js
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require('webpack/lib').container;
const name = 'B';
module.exports = {
  devtool: false,
  devServer: {
     port: 8062
  },
  output: {
    chunkLoadingGlobal: `${name}Chunks`,
    publicPath: 'auto',
    filename: `${name}/[name].js`
  },
  optimization: {
      runtimeChunk: { name: 'runtime' }
  },
  plugins: [
     new ModuleFederationPlugin({
       name: name,
       filename: `${name}/container.js`,
       runtime: 'runtime',
       remotes: {
          A:  ['A@http://0.0.0.0:8062/A/runtime.js', 'A@http://0.0.0.0:8062/A/container.js'] 
      }
    })
  ]
};
```

Now, both project A and B has own `runtime.js` and `container.js`, and run both.

If we open project A, it will load some scripts in order of `A/runtime.js`, `A/main.js`, `A/container.js`.

and in `A/main.js`, it's script look like this:


```js
(self["AChunks"] = self["AChunks"] || []).push([
["main"],

{
  "webpack/container/reference/B/fallback-1": (module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
var __webpack_error__ = new Error();
module.exports = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
	if(typeof portal !== "undefined") return resolve();
	__webpack_require__.l("http://0.0.0.0:8062/B/container.js", (event) => {
		if(typeof B !== "undefined") return resolve();
		var errorType = event && (event.type === 'load' ? 'missing' : event.type);
		var realSrc = event && event.target && event.target.src;
		__webpack_error__.message = 'Loading script failed.\n(' + errorType + ': ' + realSrc + ')';
		__webpack_error__.name = 'ScriptExternalLoadError';
		__webpack_error__.type = errorType;
		__webpack_error__.request = realSrc;
		reject(__webpack_error__);
	}, "B");
}).then(() => (B));

}),

 "webpack/container/reference/B": ((module, __unused_webpack_exports, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
var __webpack_error__ = new Error();
module.exports = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
	if(typeof B !== "undefined") return resolve();
	__webpack_require__.l("http://172.16.220.28:8066/B/runtime.js", (event) => {
		if(typeof portal !== "undefined") return resolve();
		var errorType = event && (event.type === 'load' ? 'missing' : event.type);
		var realSrc = event && event.target && event.target.src;
		__webpack_error__.message = 'Loading script failed.\n(' + errorType + ': ' + realSrc + ')';
		__webpack_error__.name = 'ScriptExternalLoadError';
		__webpack_error__.type = errorType;
		__webpack_error__.request = realSrc;
		reject(__webpack_error__);
	}, "B");
}).then(() => (B));

}),

"webpack/container/fallback/webpack/container/reference/B/and 1 more":  ((module) => {

"use strict";
var ids = ["webpack/container/reference/B","webpack/container/reference/B/fallback-1"];
var error, result, i = 0;
var loop = (next) => {
	while(i < ids.length) {
		try {
                    // Here will found function `__webpack_require__`  is not defined
                    next = __webpack_require__(ids[i++]); 
                 } catch(e) { return handleError(e); }
		if(next) return next.then ? next.then(handleResult, handleError) : handleResult(next);
	}
	if(error) throw error;
}
var handleResult = (result) => {
	if(result) return result;
	return loop();
};
var handleError = (e) => {
	error = e;
	return loop();
};
module.exports = loop();

 })
},

// webpackRuntimeModules
__webpack_require__ => {
  var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId));
  // ...
});

]);
```

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

The module `"webpack/container/fallback/webpack/container/reference/B/and 1 more"` above is loaded correctly.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

There is a way to use the runtime for both local and remote projects.

When some remote's scripts is configured as an array, it may be a good idea not to treat the latter item as a backup.

Maybe this might not be a bug, but hopefully there is the ability to allow a project that uses a single runtime to load a remote project that also uses a single runtime. If only one of them uses a single runtime, there won't be any errors here.

title: "compiling..." info is not displayed anymore after webpack-dev-server v4 release

With webpack-dev-server `3.x.x`, when running `webpack serve`, after a change done in the src code, it was possible to see in the console `compiling...`  and then `Compiled successfully` such like this:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22519163/151681881-7af890ce-26ff-420b-bf28-739e3d074851.png)

However, after the release of webpack-dev-server 4, we don't see `compiling...`  anymore, we only see `Compiled successfully`. In my opinion, it was quite useful to have `compiling...` like this we were aware that something was going on (more particularly when it is a bit long to reload).

Done in 1.06s.
```
`dist/main.mjs` uses `import` and `export`, so webpack does think, that the target environment supports EcmaScriptModule syntax.

**What is the expected behavior?**
The expected behaviour is to support ESM-output with ESM-external dependencies.  That is, webpack shall not refuse to use external type 'module', when the output is ESM, reasoning that the target does not support ESM.

```
$ /home/d/A/node_modules/.bin/webpack --version
webpack: 5.68.0
webpack-cli: 4.9.2
webpack-dev-server not installed
$ node --version
v16.13.1
```

title: working/supported image minification in webpack5 ?
body:
image minification support in webpack5 is provided by @alexander-akait 's

	ImageMinimizerWebpackPlugin
	 Plugin and Loader for webpack to optimize (compress) all images using imagemin. Do not worry about size of images, now they are always optimized/compressed.

at,

	webpack-contrib/image-minimizer-webpack-plugin
	 https://github.com/webpack-contrib/image-minimizer-webpack-plugin

image-minimizer-webpack-plugin supports only 'imagemin' & 'squoosh' minification,

	https://github.com/webpack-contrib/image-minimizer-webpack-plugin#user-content-getting-started

imagemin is unmaintained

	https://github.com/imagemin/imagemin/issues/385

and suggests,

	"I would recommend using Squoosh instead, which is actively maintained and also avoids a lot of the problems Imagemin are having with native binaries, as they're using WebAssembly."

squoosh compression fails,

	squoosh img compress ERROR: "MaxListenersExceededWarning: Possible EventEmitter memory leak detected."
	 https://github.com/GoogleChromeLabs/squoosh/issues/1206

there's been no response to that in 22 days, and the project itself looks barely active since Oct '21,

	https://github.com/GoogleChromeLabs/squoosh/commits/dev


what options for *supported* & *functional* image minification exist in the webpack5 ecosystem?

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Have a webpack config that includes this in its plugins:

```js
			new DefinePlugin({
				// defined to access the native browser import, which is ordinarily
				// prevented by (1) TS compiling import to AMD require(), and (2)

```

Upon compilation, you'll get an error

```
SyntaxError: Unexpected token (1:7)
    at Object.pp$4.raise (C:\Users\Connor\Documents\Github\vscode-dev\client\node_modules\acorn\dist\acorn.js:3460:15)
    at Object.pp$9.unexpected (C:\Users\Connor\Documents\Github\vscode-dev\client\node_modules\acorn\dist\acorn.js:756:10)

    at Object.pp$5.parseMaybeConditional (C:\Users\Connor\Documents\Github\vscode-dev\client\node_modules\acorn\dist\acorn.js:2503:2
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See above

It should not throw an error.

title: Bring back "compiling..." message
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

I'm using WebStorm, my colleagues use VSCode and run WebPack in a terminal with dev-server and hot-reload enabled. when we make changes to the files and save them, the file watcher catches the changes, says "compiling...", compiles, and then the browser reloads changed files, so far so good. But with newer versions, WebPackno longer shows the "compiling..." message. While it seems insignificant, we cannot see if the file watcher detected changes and started compiling or not, and with our build times getting longer and longer it causes a lot of frustration. Please bring back the good ol' message.

title: v5.69.0
body:
# Features * automatically switch to an ESM compatible environment when enabling ESM output mode * handle multiple alternative directories (e. g. due to `resolve.alias` or `resolve.modules`) when creating an context module * add `util/types` to node.js built-in modules * add `__webpack_exports_info__..canMangle` api # Bugfixes * fix bug in chunk graph generation which leads to modules being included in chunk desprite them being already included in parent chunks * avoid writing more than 2GB at once during cache serialization (as workaround for node.js/libuv bug on MacOS) * fix handling of whitespaces in semver ranges when using Module Federation * avoid generating hashes which contain only numbers as they likely conflict with module ids * fix resource name based placeholders for data uris * fix cache serialization for context elements * fix passing of `stage` option when instrumenting plugins for the ProfilingPlugin * fix tracking of declarations in concatenated modules to avoid conflicts * fix unstable mangling of exports * fix handling of `#` in paths of loaders * avoid unnecessary cache update when using `experiments.buildHttp` # Contributing * update typescript and jest # Developer Experience * expose some additional typings for usage in webpack-cli
This discussion was created from the release v5.69.0.

title: Intermittent chunk load errors in Firefox
body: # Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Intermittently, Firefox users of our Angular site (with lazy loading of chunks) will get ChunkLoadErrors that prevent our site from loading properly. This has been maddeningly intermittent and I have not been able to reproduce it, but I've collected some HAR files from users with details that may point in the right direction. This may be an issue with Firefox itself, or possibly plugins/add-ons/extension (although from the list of plugins users have reported, there is no plugin in common between them all, I believe, and some have said they have seen the issue when they run vanilla Firefox.) I'm not super adept at decoding HAR files, but it is interesting is that when chunks fail to load, there appears to be something odd with the Protocol. First, a chunk that loaded properly (it seems): Now, from the same HAR, a chunk that did not load properly (notice status code 0, -1 B transferred). Notice also that the GET line in the raw Request omits HTTP/2. I believe that this file was the missing chunk that broke the page: But also from the same HAR, there is this request, that seems to have loaded properly, but for some reason has status code 0? This one could just be loading from Cache or something, but I'm not sure. In all cases, it seem that users are able to fix the problem by clearing their caches and reloading the page. From the HAR files, I've seen that the filenames after reload are the same as those that failed to load previously, so we aren't dealing new code being deployed resulting in missing old chunks. For a bit more background with some additional HAR files, see [this StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71070943/what-is-the-source-of-this-intermittent-webpack-chunkloaderror-in-firefox). This has also been discussed at length on our site's forums, [here](https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/2788693/specific-videos-page-immediately-redirects-me-empt) and [here](https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/2800974/cant-open-forum-threads-firefox). **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** I suppose, load one of the threads linked to our site above and cross your fingers, but I've never been able to reproduce the issue myself. **What is the expected behavior?** Chunks should all load. **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.67.0 Node.js version: 14.18.1 Operating System: Additional tools: Angular-CLI I have quite a few more HAR files, but I want to be careful how I share the data from them, because they contain some sensitive information.
author: karptonite

title: Tree-shaking doesn't work on re-exported modules with classes
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
If you have reexported class without usage it's not removed from production build

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
1. Create class `export default class WontUse extends HTMLElement {
  static SomeStaticProp = "Tree-shaking doesn't work";
}
`  into wontUse.ts file
2. Create file `reexport.ts` with `export { default as WontUse } from "./wontUse";` and some default function for future usage
3. Import default function from `reexport.ts` and call it in `main.ts`
4. Build project and you can notice that WontUse class included in build-file despite it doesn't in use at all
You can check easily here: https://github.com/Yegorich555/webpack-must-have/tree/bug_treeShake
just clone and run `npm i && npm run build-prod`

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
All useless classes (not only functions and variables) must be excluded from build-files


<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.67.0 
Node.js version: 14.19.0
Operating System: Windows 10
Additional tools: VSCode

title: failed to inference ONNX model: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'InferenceSession')

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->


<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->


Two weeks ago I opened a thread in `StackOverFlow` asking for help about `ONNX` not "read"/"loaded" by `webpack`:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71067989/failed-to-inference-onnx-model-typeerror-cannot-read-properties-of-undefined

but didn't receive any help.


<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->


I wrote a `GitHub's issue` in the Microsoft's ONNX page: https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/issues/10516
and, according to them : https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/issues/10516#issuecomment-1049295718 
, in order to be able to load `ONNX` through `webpack`, the `webpack.config.js` should have these traits:
`onnxruntime-web` NOT in `externals` , `target`: `web`,  and `NO alias`.




**What is the current behavior?**


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

title: Module Federation in Production
body:
I'm curious if anyone can share their experiences using module federation in production. Are there any known public success stories of moving to module federation?

The feature has been available for some time now, so I assume it is stable, but how widely used is it in practice?

For anyone who has used it in production, I would love to hear about the pros and cons of your experiences. What has worked out, and what were some unexpected downsides?

title: v5.70.0
body:
# Features * update node.js version constraints for ESM support * add `baseUri` to `entry` options to configure a static base uri (the base of `new URL()`) * alphabetically sort exports in namespace objects when possible * add `__webpack_exports_info__.name.canMangle` * add proxy support to `experiments.buildHttp` * `import.meta.webpackContext` as ESM alternative to `require.context` * handle multiple alternative directories (e. g. due to resolve.alias or resolve.modules) when creating an context module # Bugfixes * fix problem when assigning `global` to a variable * fix crash when using `experiments.outputModule` and `loaderContext.importModule` with multiple chunks * avoid generating progress output before the compilation has started (ProgressPlugin) * fix handling of non-static-ESM dependencies with using TLA and HMR in the same module * include the asset module filename in hashing * `output.clean` will keep HMR assets for at least 10s to allow HMR to access them even when compilation is faster then the browser # Performance * fix asset caching when using the BannerPlugin # Developer Experience * improve typings # Contributing * capture caching errors when running the test suite
This discussion was created from the release v5.70.0.

title: How to get specific source code before buildModule hook
body:
I am creating a webpack-plugin. There is a scenario where a CSS file is dynamically created based on a specific source code (e.g., .tsx). The creation of the CSS file is done once with all source code combined. This means that sequential execution is not possible for each source code fetch. The CSS file is then written out as a virtual module. Is there a good way to accomplish this scenario? In The `buildModule` hook, `RawSource` does not exist. ( I don't want to read file) The `succeedModule` hook has a `RawSource`, but it is already built, so updates to the virtual module will not be reflected. --- Reference The example is an entry point, importing a virtual module. ```index.ts // index.ts import "map.css" import A from "A.tsx" import B from "B.tsx" ```` ```A.tsx // A.tsx export default A = () =>
A
``` ```B.tsx // B.tsx export default B = () =>
B
``` The virtual module `map.css` is output with the classes extracted from the `.tsx` file mapped as styles. ```map.css .block{display:block}.inline-block{display:inline-block} ``` [mapcss](https://github.com/TomokiMiyauci/mapcss) maps classes to CSS Statements.

title: ensure identical chunk-hashes and chunk-ids on different platforms
body:
Using webpack on different platforms to bundle an application results in different hashes and in some instances even different chunk ids. The respective portion of our webpack configuration looks as follows:
```ts
output: {
    filename: '[name].[contenthash].js',
    chunkFilename: '[name].[contenthash].chunk.js',
    ...
},
```
The hashes on linux and mac are identical. On windows they differ. We found out that the only thing inside the generated chunks that differs is the order of the keys of the module objects. Any idea on how to ensure identical hashes for identical sources regardless of the platform the code is bundled on?

title: Multiple entry points - how to keep one as small as possible?
body:
I've got a single-page application that shows a splash screen while the app loads. You can see it [here.](https://lyricistant.app). Due to naming being hard, that splash screen is named `preload`, while my actual application is named `main`. My webpack config looks a bit like this: ```js { entry: { preload: './src/preload.js', main: './src/main.js', }, } ``` My HTML looks a bit like this: ```html
``` I've recently come to find out that since I'm including two entries in the same HTML file, I should _actually_ be setting `optimization.runtimeChunk` to `single` to avoid both of these entry chunks unnecessarily including the runtime. However, when I do that, my `dist/preload.js` balloons from 6kb to 30kb. Not a _huge_ gain but I want to keep that `dist/preload.js` as **small** as possible, since I need it to load first before my app can display anything. Honestly, 6kb is already a lot for my `dist/preload.js` as my `src/preload.js` has 2 import: 1 that is a very simple function that returns a basic object and depends on no other imports, and a 2kb (unminified) SVG that's loaded with raw loader. The function IS used across both my preload and main entry points, though. Still, I was expecting more like 3kb as the code is so minimal but 6 has been good enough. It seems like I've been getting lucky that my preload has even come out to 6kb; when I look at it, I see that it contains some code that my preload entry point never imports or uses, and that I know the single function I import never uses. Namely, it creates some symbols for various React stuff and some mappings of enum names to values. Anyway, what I'm wondering is this: is there something I can change about my Webpack config to both take advantage of `runtimeChunks: 'single'` while also telling Webpack directly (instead of just getting lucky) that it should try and keep `dist/preload.js` as small as it possible can? I've tried `optimization.splitChunks.maxSize`, `optimization.splitChunks.maxInitialSize`, and `optimization.concatenateModules` to no avail. Note: Everything here is a simplified version of my actual application but I've tried to be as detailed as possible while leaving out the irrelevant complexities of my application. However, my application is open-source and the code for it is [available here.](https://github.com/wardellbagby/lyricistant)

author: austinjbennett

title: Does it make sense to bring back CommonsChunkPlugin?
body:
Hello,

**TL;DR: Would it be possible/feasible to bring back `CommonsChunkPlugin` as an optional alternative to `SplitChunksPlugin` ?**

I'm working in an enormous code base that `SplitChunksPlugin` doesn't seem to handle very well.  Or, at least, I haven't been able to find a SplitChunks configuration that works well for us, despite literally dozens of hours spent debugging into the plugin and analyzing the internals.  No matter what I do, our build output always has:

* Lots of modules duplicated across many chunks
* Too many (relatively) small chunks

In Webpack 3, we had things working pretty well by using `CommonsChunkPlugin` in combination with some coarse-grained "webpackChunkName" magic comments in dynamic imports that were structured such that we (a) had a relatively small number of well-named chunks, and (b) had a single parent chunk that all other chunks were children of.

Organizing the async chunks like this, of course, made for a lot of duplicated modules.  To resolve this we relied on 2 plugins:

1. **RemoveParentModulesPlugin**.  If a module exists in all parents of a chunk, it will be removed from the child chunk.
2. **CommonsChunkPlugin**.  For each functional area, we extracted duplicated modules into a well-named "functional-area-common" chunk.  We also, as the final invocation of this plugin, extracted modules duplicated across functional areas or in the main parent chunk into a top-level "common" chunk.

This approach relied on the fact that Webpack executes `RemoveParentModulesPlugin` before every separate instance of `CommonsChunkPlugin`.  So after a new "functional-area-common" chunk is created as a child of the main parent chunk, the next invocation of `RemoveParentModulesPlugin` will have a  new chance to push the modules up into the parent.

This approach was pretty easy to understand, reason about and control, and it seemed to work well.  The loaded chunks were well-named and coarse-grained and the duplication was pretty minimal.  Indeed we ended up with a very large main parent chunk (and associated common chunk) but that is fine because that code is needed by all workflows and should be always be loaded.  The number of requests at runtime was fairly low.

With Webpack 5 and `SplitChunksPlugin`, I've become convinced after an enormous effort that there is no way to replicate this behaviour.  The new heuristics-based approach is very difficult to reason about in a large code base and even with explicitly named cache groups I can't get close to what we had before.  Even with debugging, I honestly can't predict (or even understand in some cases) which configuration changes are going to control what output.  Configuration is a lot of guesswork and when you're dealing with 100K modules, there's really no way to guess right.

Additionally, the iterative effect I described above between `RemoveParentModulesPlugin` and `CommonsChunkPlugin` is no longer there because all cache groups are handled inside a single `SplitChunksPlugin` invocation.  This means there's only a single `RemoveParentModulesPlugin` invocation and a single `SplitChunksPlugin` invocation.

Which bring me to my question. 😄 

Would it be possible/feasible to bring back `CommonsChunkPlugin` as a possible (of course, optional) alternative to `SplitChunksPlugin` ?  I guess it was removed because it was felt that the functionality was superseded by SplitChunks, but that's clearly not the case for all kinds of code bases.  Maybe having a choice here would be better?

If you're still with me at this point, thanks for your attention. 😄

title: Forcing crossOrigin for same-origin request in LoadScriptRuntimeModule
body:
I have encountered a bug in angular when running inside an Android WebView.

After investigating I found out the scripts with type="module" do not send credentials by default, even for the same origin. This naturally breaks the angular application since the scripts fail to load.

There is a build option that forces adding the crossOrigin for the initial scripts, however, this doesn't work for lazy loaded scripts.

The answer I got from the Angular team is that it cannot be done  because it is a webpack mechanism. Following is the final answer, followed by a link to the issue.

Angular Team Answer:
This is not actionable from our end as that is how Webpack handles crossOrigin. https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/c181294865dca01b28e6e316636fef5f2aad4eb6/lib/runtime/LoadScriptRuntimeModule.js#L89-L96

Issue:
https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/22942#issue-1192905676


Does anyone know how this can be achieved ? 
For WebView to work a crossOrigin must be added for type="module" scripts, even inside the same origin.

Right now I manually edit the runtime.js file after creation to remove the same origin check.

title: How to work with toConstantDependency function when the second argument of that function includes a certain member imported with import statement
body:
As the title above says, how to resolve the specified use case? Here is a short(or simplified) example about more detail information. The followings are a bunch of code snippets.

First, I would like to achieve a certain feature, which is to replace some text during compilation stage via webpack plugin.

```js
import time from 'time'

// replace some patterned templates like `__(foo)` with some translations
// in the following example, `__('Time is ' + time)` is replaced with `'время ' + time` during compilation stage
__('Time is ' + time)
```

Here is a certain code snippet about webpack plugin
```js
// replacement is a string like 'time' but the raw format of replacement argument is a variable imported from other module using import statement
toConstantDependency(parser, replacement)(callExpressionNode)
```

After run some bootstrap script like 'npm start', The compilation process is pass and generate a lot of assets. But the web app is not working. After debugging, I think that `toConstantDependency` seems to be a root cause as it cannot work well with imported module using import statement. Finally, I find a workaround that is using a temporary variable to take imported variable like the following.

```js
import oldTime from 'time'

const time = oldTime 

// replace some patterned templates like `__(foo)` with some translations
// in the following example, `__('Time is ' + time)` is replaced with `'время ' + time` during compilation stage
__('Time is ' + time)
```

title: SSR and lazyCompilation
body:
Currently, we heavily use the SSR by building both client & server using webpack's MultiCompiler. In order to get the required scripts and styles from the SSR we read the client's compilation stats.json and use libs such as [loadable components](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/7c6052a084dca523b4f60f8132e67daecd587407/packages/next/server/dev/on-demand-entry-handler.ts#L0-L1)

It will be great to use such a compiler option as `lazyCompilation` that may bring performance improvements. But, it seems it currently doesn't support the SSR well as we cannot gather info about used chunks on the page as they are generated dynamically by webpack and it leads to page flashes due to empty css on page loading.

Does someone has any working example for the SSR and lazyImport or have any advice on how to use both in development without losing SSR features?

title: `optimization.concatenateModules` leading to nondeterministic build output
body:
I recently upgraded to webpack 5, and I'm noticing that our builds no longer have deterministic outputs. I've narrowed down the issue to `optimization.concatenateModules` – when I set it to `false`, I get the same output on every build, although I'd like to keep `concatenateModules` on. With it on, the output just differs slightly. It looks like the modules are being concatenated in different orders? For example:

```
-        Et = new pt(Ct),
-        kt = new ht(Ct);
+        Et = new ht(Ct),
+        kt = new pt(Ct);
```

Any idea what might be causing this? Not sure if it's a bug in the `ModuleConcatenationPlugin`, but given that I haven't seen any other issues about this or any mention of this problem online, I figure maybe there's something in our configuration that could be causing this?

title: How to solve the problem that fsevents.node in chokidar is compiled during the compilation phase of webpack production packaging?
body: ## Feature request have a way to ignore require calls! **What is the expected behavior?** **What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?** The thing is this, I use electron-reload in the main process typescript file of electron to do hot loading, electron-reload depends on chokidar (fsevents.node) and needs to use the node-loader module to compile, but I only want to use it in the development environment used in , so I wrote it like this: And also set the node-loader to be used in the development environment. However, due to its typescript static ES module compilation, chokidar (fsevents.node) is still introduced at compile time. At this time, there is no node-loader module for compilation. Is there any way I can do it without using node-loader? Next, ignore electron-reload, I need this function urgently. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11425476/169498324-b9478afa-4c6b-4c23-8027-d45082b24027.png) When node-loader is not used in production environment. **How should this be implemented in your opinion?** Perhaps,like webpack-node-externals, I can specify which modules you want to ignore **Are you willing to work on this yourself?** yes
author: white-than-wood

title: Chunking seems not to be working when importing from packages in monorepo
body:
I'm trying to figure this out since days now but I simply can't get my head around what's going on. I further don't know if this problem is **babel** related or if it originates from **webpack**.

I have a monorepo with **3 apps** which all rely on components from `packages`
```
frontend/
  apps/       <-- each app is a package in the scope of the Monroe
    app1/
    ...
  packages/   <-- this is a package in the monorepo scope
    components/
    providers/
    ...
```

Inside _app specific_ components I import eg. components like
```tsx
import { MyComponent } from 'packages/components';
```
Inside _packages_ I have aliased imports, eg. requesting a _component_ in _providers_
```tsx
import { MyComponent } from 'frontend/components';
```

The `tsconfig.json` in **packages** defines the local aliases
```
{
  ...
  "paths": {
    "frontend/components": ["./components"],
    ...
}
```

In my `webpack.config.ts` I define the aliases like
```ts
{
  resolve: {
    alias: {
      components: resolve(__dirname, "../../../frontend/apps/campus/src/components") // <-- resembles the absolute path to the config file
    }
  }
}
```

It all works but when I check my bundle I get a **main chunk** with the entire content from **packages**? Does that have something to do with my aliases or should I do something with my chunk settings? 

Here's a screenshot from the bundle analyzer:
![Screenshot 2022-05-20 at 13 18 16](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9102922/169517177-c01daa6b-e5f1-476a-b9ff-29ea0e183ed3.png)

Especially interesting is the `iconpack_default`, from which I **dynamically import** icons. I would have expected this to put every icon inside a separate chunk...

I know, this might be quite vague but I'd appreciate any kind of push in the right direction!

Thanks! 🙌

const name = "BrowserReloadErrorOverlayWepbackPlugin";

class BrowserReloadErrorOverlayWepbackPlugin {
  constructor(options) {

    };
  }

  async start() {
    const { options } = this;

    // 设置静态服务器
    portfinder.basePort = port;
    port = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      //查找端口号
      portfinder.getPort((err, port) => {
        if (err) {
          reject(err);
          return;
        }
        // 新端口
        resolve(port);
      });
    });

    this.wss = new WebSocket.Server({ port });

    this.wss.on("connection", function (ws) {
      // 远程socket
      // this.broadcast(
      //   JSON.stringify({
      //     cmd: "cmd:reload",
      //     message: "",
      //   })
      // );
    });

    this.clientSrc = createClient({
      port,
      retryWait: options.retryWait,
      delay: options.delay,
    });
  }

  broadcast(message) {
    this.wss.clients.forEach((client) => client.send(message));
  }
  // // 做兼容
  hook(compiler, hookName, pluginName, fn) {
    if (arguments.length === 3) {
      fn = pluginName;
      pluginName = hookName;
    }
    if (compiler.hooks) {
      compiler.hooks[hookName].tap(pluginName, fn);
    } else {
      compiler.plugin(pluginName, fn);
    }
  }
  apply(compiler) {
    const { options, clientSrc } = this;
    const { compilerWatch = () => {} } = options;
    // // // 开始编译 只会调用一次
    this.hook(compiler, "afterPlugins", async (compilation) => {
      if (!this.wss) {
        await this.start();
      }
    });

    // const matchObject = ModuleFilenameHelpers.matchObject.bind(undefined, {
    //   include: options.include || /\.js$/,
    //   exclude: options.exclude,
    // });

 
    compiler.watch(
      {
        // [watchOptions](/configuration/watch/#watchoptions) 示例
        // aggregateTimeout: 300,
        poll: undefined,
      },
      async (err, stats) => {
        compilerWatch(err, stats);
        
        this.broadcast(
          JSON.stringify({
            cmd: "cmd:rebuilding",
            message: "",
          })
        );
        // stabilization(500).then(() => {
        if (err) {
          this.broadcast(
            JSON.stringify({
              cmd: "cmd:buildError",
              message: err.stack || err || err.details,
            })
          );
          // console.log('Errors:' + chalk.red(err.stack || err));
          // if (err.details) {
          //     console.log('Errors:' + chalk.red(err.details));
          // }
          return;
        }

        if (stats.hasErrors()) {
          this.broadcast(
            JSON.stringify({
              cmd: "cmd:buildError",
              message:
                stats.toString({
                  colors: true,
                }) + "\n\n",
            })
          );
          // console.log(
          //     'Errors:' +
          //         chalk.red(
          //             stats.toString({
          //                 colors: true,
          //             }) + '\n\n'
          //         )
          // );
          return;
        } else if (stats.hasWarnings()) {
          // console.log(
          //   "Warnings:" +
          //     chalk.yellow(
          //       stats.toString({
          //         colors: true,
          //       }) + "\n\n"
          //     )
          // );
          // return;
        }

        this.broadcast(
          JSON.stringify({
            cmd: "cmd:reload",
            message: "",
          })
        );
        // else {
        //     process.stdout.write(
        //         stats.toString({
        //             colors: true,
        //         }) + '\n\n'
        //     );
        // }
      }
    );

    compiler.hooks.emit.tap("ErrorOverlayWebpack", (compilation) => {
      const { clientSrc } = this;
      for (const name in compilation.assets) {
        if (compilation.assets.hasOwnProperty(name) && name.endsWith(".js")) {
          const contents = compilation.assets[name].source();
          const withoutComments =
            ansiToHtml + "\n" + clientSrc + "\n" + contents;
          compilation.assets[name] = {
            source: () => withoutComments,
            size: () => withoutComments.length,
          };
        }
      }
    });
  }
}

title: Webworker and style loader - causing load issues with babylon js inspector
body:
This is likely a non-standard use case - however the original disussion between myself and babylonjs devs is here: [js forums](https://forum.babylonjs.com/t/importing-inspector-in-typescript-cause-failure-if-engine-started-with-offscreen-canvas/19234/9)

The summary is that babylonJS has a component that can be imported via a simple `import 'script'` call, but that doesn't actually get enabled unless you then call an actual command there after. The code i am using is designed to be aware of if you are running normally, or if it is inside a webworker, but because the component is added via typescript import i'm fairly sure i cannot have runtime conditionals on the import statement.

When this component is added, the webpack style-loader component is run and it tries to access the DOM to inject various stylings which ultimately are not used. a minimal reproduction of this issue is here: [github project](https://github.com/rdpeake/babylon-inspector-repro/tree/master) though the project hasn't been updated the related issues persist with the latest versions.

my question is, is there a change that either babylonjs devs, or webpack devs can make such that style-loader is dom aware, or is there something i can edit to achieve this. my current solution is to patch styleDomAPI locally to be a noop if it has no document object.

title: How can I bypass a package's `exports` configuration and import arbitrary files?
body:
I'm using a third-party package that doesn't expose all its internal files through the `exports` field on `package.json`. I'd like to import an internal file from this package, but webpack (correctly!) respects the defined exports in the `exports` field. I know that I can completely disable support for the `exports` field by setting `config.resolve.exportsFields` to an empty array, but how can I either disable `exports` for this singular third-party dependency or bypass the `exports` config for a singular file import from said dependency?

For example, let's say the third-party package `super-rad` has the following in `package.json`:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": "./main.js",
    "./sub/path": "./secondary.js",
    "./prefix/": "./directory/",
    "./prefix/deep/": "./other-directory/",
    "./other-prefix/*": "./yet-another/*/*.js"
  }
}
```

If I want to use `import thing from 'super-rad/internal/constants'`, how would I do that without completely disabling support for `exports` fields in other packages?

title: `webpack-dev-server`: HMR doesn't work unless `liveReload` is explicitly disabled
body:
I'd like to preface this by saying this issue stems from something encountered in [`preact-cli`](https://github.com/preactjs/preact-cli). This is a rather big and complicated Webpack config, so I don't want to waste anyone's time in trying to poke around in it. I'm writing this in the hopes that this might be an obvious issue to someone as days of poking around have achieved **a patch that works**, though I'm confused as to why.

## Summary

We've ran into a rather confusing issue upon updating `webpack-dev-serrver` to v4, in that even with `devServer: { hot: true }`, HMR will not work unless `liveReload` is explicitly disabled, i.e., `devServer: { hot: true, liveReload: false }`.

This doesn't seem like the correct behavior according to [the docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/dev-server/#devserverlivereload), but as I've failed to create a minimal reproduction, I would assume something else is going on here outside of the dev server config.

## Reproduction

A reproduction of this can be found here: https://github.com/rschristian/webpack-dev-server-hmr-issue, instructions are in the ReadMe.

## Closing

Does anyone have an idea on what could cause this? `liveReload` shouldn't take effect if `hot` is enabled according to the docs, but it seems to take effect _despite_ `hot` being enabled. Our `devServer` config can be found here: https://github.com/preactjs/preact-cli/blob/a60f8df8e87935f3b3e03a2bb4aecda801568c83/packages/cli/lib/lib/webpack/webpack-client-config.js#L333-L356

Again, it's a big config and we do have a working solution, so I don't want to waste any maintainer's time on this if something isn't immediately obvious. I'm not sure if this is a bug or some weird config we have going on.

author: beumsk

title: Which combinations of "output.target" and "output.library.type" are being frequently used in Webpack configuration?
body:
I suppose not all combinations of `output.target` and `output.library.type` are valid and/or meaningful. Which ones are meaningful and being frequently used?

## From the viewpoint of logic

### Case 1: Library for Browser JS planned to be imported by Webpack 

* Obviously, the **target** must be `web`
* **output.library.type** must be the ... `module`, I suppose, because using Webpack we don't need to take the functionality from the global scope (`window`).
* For TypeScript case: the `modules` should be set to `ES6` or above to enable the [tree shaking](https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/) functionality on production mode


## Case 2: Web library which intended to be accessible via `script` tag

* Obviously, the **target** must be `web` 
* **output.library.type** must be the ... `window`, I suppose.


## Case 3: NodeJS library

Altgough the develpment team of Node.js seriously improved the ECMAScript modules support,
I suppose it is too early (for 2022) to tell about ECMAScript modules as main modules of Node.js.

* Obviously, the `target` must be the `node`. 
* **output.library.type** must be the ... `commonjs`, I suppose, but there are similar `commonjs2` and `commonjs-module` there. It will take a lot of time to explore the differences in details, so I hope on your reccomendations.


### Case 4: The library which could be used by both BrowserJS and Node.js

* The The [Authoring Libraries](https://webpack.js.org/guides/author-libraries/) guide does not chage the default `web` value of the `target`, thus, it will be `web` even if the library will be used in Node.js environment.
* The [Authoring Libraries](https://webpack.js.org/guides/author-libraries/) guide reccomends "umd" as `library.type`. But... will tree shaking available with this type at least, for web. Afraid, no. Is so, it's better to use multicompilation with different target environments.

But: it was the rare case where UMD was usefull for me.
I tried to build the JavaScript for... Pug pre-processor, which has internal JavaScript environment. It does not understand neither `import` nor `require`, so `umd` was a solution there.

## Remarks

* `libraryTarget` is planned to drop in the future.
*  I am asking this question here after [has not been answered on Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72723248/which-combinations-of-output-target-and-output-library-type-are-being-freque) even with bounty.

title: Custom runtime chunk loader (because urls are unknown during build time)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

Load chunks from locations, which are unavailable during build time.

When uploading JavaScript assets to Hubspot CMS, the files are automatically minified, post-processed and pushed to a CDN, for caching reason Hubspot delivers each asset **from a different location**. So I am looking for a solution to load chunks from a location, which is **not known during build time**.

Find more details at the end.

**What is the expected behavior?**

Possibility to inject a custom chunk loader which provided the correct filename without the need of hacking the created JavaScript files.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

During upload to Hubspot, JavaScript files are post processed and served from different locations for caching reasons.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Unclear.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

## More details

when uploading JavaScript assets to Hubspot CMS, the files are automatically minified, post-processed and pushed to a cdn, for caching reason Hubspot delivers each asset from a different location. So I am looking for a solution to load chunks from a location, which is not known during build time.

**Locally**

 - js/widget.js
 - js/41.widget.js
 - js/393.widget.js

**Remote**

- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/50239061735/1656576587321/index.js
- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/50238026977/1656576587779/41.widget.js
- http://f.hubspotusercontent-eu1.net/hub/xxx/hub_generated/template_assets/unknown/location/393.widget.js

And here the challenge starts, as 41.widget.js or 393.widget.js cannot be loaded, as they are not within the same url as index.js. Unfortunately, the final urls are not available during build time, the are created during upload to Hubspot.

During the upload to hubspot, some placeholders within the JavaScript assets are replaced (Hubspot Template language HubL). So, if I manage to define the output.publicPath **for each chunk** as ``https://{{get_asset_url('./js/<chunk>.js')}}`` Hubspot could replace this by the correct url. 

I tried to manually patch the created index.js which does the trick, but seems a bit risky for me, unstable and hard to automate.

```
!function () {

    /* much more */

    o.u = function (e) {
        console.log("Load chunk");
        console.log(e);
        var result = "";
        if (e === 41) result = "{{get_asset_url('/theme/js/widgets/41.widgets.js')}}";
        if (e === 393) result = "{{get_asset_url('/theme/js/widgets/393.widgets.js')}}";
        console.log(result);
        return result;

    }, o.p = "https://", 

    /* much more */
}();

```

title: Trying to ignore some css line with a custom Hubspot selector
body:
Hello,

I'm trying to deploy an Hubspot theme built with Tailwind, I've decided to add Webpack + PostCss to allow for auto build of TailwindCSS + Import + SCSS support.

However I'm stuck with an issue:

Hubspot requires us to use the get_asset_url selector in css when you need to access an asset for background image for example.

So my CSS file (before process) will look like this:

`  .page-header-text-title {
    background-image: url("{{ get_asset_url('../images/page-name-doodle.svg') }}");
  }
`

Obviously this will crash as MiniCssExtractPlugin cannot process this line.

`ERROR in ./src/css/styles.css
Module build failed (from ./node_modules/mini-css-extract-plugin/dist/loader.js):
HookWebpackError: Cannot find module '{{ get_asset_url('../images/page-name-doodle.svg') }}'`

I'm trying to figure what can I do to fix this, and maybe ignore this line when processing the file or creating a custom rule to ignore this.

Does someone know how to do this?

I've been trying for a few hours to find a good solution but cannot figure this out.

Thanks !

title: How can I achieve tree shaking in development mode?
body:
Hi everyone, I'm posting this with the hope that I'll get some help, as I've searched and researched a lot of websites without finding a working fix.

So, we have a monorepo with Lerna that includes different packages. Two of the most important ones are React components packages.
Let's call them `package-a` and `package-b`.
`package-b` is the newer one and depends on `package-a`, by importing some of its components and customizing them, according to the needs.

In short, this is the project structure:

```
packages
|__ **package-a**
|____ node_modules
|____ package.json
|____ webpack.config.js
|____ src
|_______ atoms
|__________ index.js
|__________ button.js
|__________ avatar.js
|_______ molecules
|__________ index.js
|__________ slider.js
|_______ index.js (exports all the components from the above folders (atoms & molecules))
|____ other-files

|__ **package-b**
|____ node_modules
|_______ package-a
|_______ other-node-modules
|____ package.json
|____ webpack.config.js
|____ src
|_______ atoms
|__________ index.js
|__________ different-button.js (here we can import the button from package-a, for example)
|_______ molecules
|__________ different-slider.js (here we can import the slider from package-a, for example)
|_______ index.js (exports all the components from atoms & molecules)
|____ other-files

|__ css-package
|__ svg-package
|__ other-packages
```

The problem is, I noticed that `package-b`'s bundle size is pretty big in development. If `package-a`'s size is around 10MB, `package-b`'s size is double. This created a bit of a headache when working with these packages, as the load time is quite big.

I noticed that in `package-b`'s bundle size, we get `package-a` bundle included, even if we import a single component from there. I tried different tips I've found while researching tree shaking, but nothing seems to bring the size down. Anyone can help me please?

title: v5.74.0
body:
# Features * add `resolve.extensionAlias` option which allows to alias extensions * This is useful when you are forced to add the `.js` extension to imports when the file really has a `.ts` extension (typescript + `"type": "module"`) * add support for ES2022 features like static blocks * add Tree Shaking support for `ProvidePlugin` # Bugfixes * fix persistent cache when some build dependencies are on a different windows drive * make order of evaluation of side-effect-free modules deterministic between concatenated and non-concatenated modules * remove left-over from debugging in TLA/async modules runtime code * remove unneeded extra 1s timestamp offset during watching when files are actually untouched * This sometimes caused an additional second build which are not really needed * fix `shareScope` option for `ModuleFederationPlugin` * set `"use-credentials"`` also for same origin scripts # Performance * Improve memory usage and performance of aggregating needed files/directories for watching * This affects rebuild performance # Extensibility * export `HarmonyImportDependency` for plugins
This discussion was created from the release v5.74.0.

title: Is there a way to trees shake exports from a file from the context of a chunk
body:
A react project at my org has a pattern where a single file does a lot of re-exporting. What I noticed is that all the exports and their dependencies go into the initial chunk because it uses a few of these exports. Most of the re-exports are actually used by dynamically loaded modules and are not needed in most cases but still are loaded every time because they are part of the entry chunk.
I have created a [sandbox to demo](https://codesandbox.io/s/dependencies-escaping-async-chunk-tu12ey?file=/src/utils.js).

Here the blueMethod is only needed for the async chunk but is still added to the initial chunk. 

Is there a way we can optimize such cases without refactoring the file into smaller files? may be by eliminating the exports not used by the particular chunk?
author: AmalJossy

title: Is there a way to modify webpack chunk name in user's browser
body:
i have a react application in `webpack` setting the chunks are getting generated like  

    50b74e3a.chunk.js

every time its got build the hash changed by `webpack `but that is not useful for cache busting 

in browser , there its cached the old chunk file even after chunk hash changed on server 

so for that i am thinking is there a way to change

    ` 50b74e3a.chunk.js`

 to    
  

   ` 50b74e3a.chunk.js?timestamp=browserUserTime`  so this should come from user's machine

by this way it will be changed by client `Javacsript `at loading time which means it will have to load the fresh copy from server

title: Decision for dependencies and devdependencies
body:
From this:
https://jsramblings.com/do-dependencies-devdependencies-matter-when-using-webpack/
It seems that when we use webpack it doesn't matter where to put our packages, either in dependencies or devdependencies it will be the same, webpack will bundle what is needed.
But for licenses issue, I want to know the exact place where to put each needed package , so the things that are not bundled I will add them in devdependencies.
I am thinking to make my decision upon theses rules:
1- Use the result from : https://github.com/webpack-contrib/webpack-bundle-analyzer each packages is in the stat will be treated as a dependency.
2- Use the npm convention, when they do not use --dev for npm install, it will be treated as a dependency.
3- Any module where we had to "import" it or "require" it, will be considered as a dependency.

Do you think that is enough ? also what about the dependencies of some of my devdependencies? for example babel/runtime is bundled (webpack bundle analyzer shows that) but this module is not mentioned in my package.json , it is mentioned as a dependency for a devdependency

title: [Discussion] How to estimate the time complexity of the webpack build/compilation?
body:
<!-- Bug reports and Feature requests must use other templates, or will be closed -->
<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter (https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack). -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
Let's say we use typescript on a project, it has 1000 lines of code, and there have 10 esmodule dependencies, and we assume the base compilation complexity of the typescript loader is `n`, and the per dependency bundle is `m`, can we estimate a approximate time complexity of webpack build this project to a single js file? Is there a formula like `<line of code> * <loader complexity> + <number of dependecies> * <bundle complexity>` so the final time complexity of this project would be `1000 * n + 10 * m`
author: suguanYang

title: A linked dep's re-build while dev server is running causes TypeScript/Webpack to not be able to find the dep anymore
body:
So I have a monorepo using yarn 3.x workspaces and there's a package with webpack that depends on an other package which is symlinked through yarn. The problem is that while a webpack dev server is running if I re-build that linked dependency, webpack is no longer able to find that dependency, even after the linked rebuild has finished and there are definitely files there.

I originally thought this is a stale cache issue relating to cache-loader, but the issue persists even after removing cache-loader. It seems as if webpack is caching the fact that the linked package can't be found during the rebuild and no longer tries to look for it once the rebuild has finished. 

> TS2307: Cannot find module 'package' or its corresponding type declarations.

Any ideas? 

Here's a reproduction link: https://github.com/specklesystems/speckle-server/pull/889
1. Checkout the branch
2. Run `yarn dev:frontend` from repo root to run the webpack dev server
3. Once its running, run `yarn dev` from 'packages/viewer'
4. The frontend dev server will rebuild, but wont be able to find the linked `viewer` dependency anymore

title: Using Webpack with Template Engine
body:
Many years ago I used Yeoman + Grunt + [Assemble.IO](https://assemble.io/) to create static websites with templates. I created a template with the top menu, footer, and other things that would be used on all pages (layout.hbs) and then individually created each page I needed using a .hbs file, eg contact.hbs, index.hbs, signup.hbs, downloads.hbs, about.hbs, etc.

When I compiled with Grunt, all these files were merged together with the layout.hbs, which resulted in HTML files, ready to be uploaded to the production servers.

Unfortunately all these libraries have become outdated with time and I am studying working with Webpack and Handlebar.

My question is: Can webpack + handlebar (or other plugin) have this behavior of generating an HTML file based on a base template + partial page?

If so, can anyone recommend a complete tutorial that teaches how to use it?
