title: Why is the mode option value injected as process.env.NODE_ENV value?
body:
### scripts

```json
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cross-env NODE_ENV=local webpack serve --mode=development",
    "start:dev": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development webpack serve  --mode=development",
    "build": " cross-env NODE_ENV=production webpack --progress --mode=production",
    "build:local": " cross-env NODE_ENV=local webpack --progress --mode=development",
    "build:dev": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development webpack --progress --mode=development",
    "storybook": "start-storybook -p 6006",
    "build-storybook": "build-storybook"
  },
```

In this way, I am injecting the process.env.NODE_ENV value into the environment where the webpack command is executed.



In my React app code, I want to look up the value injected by scripts with process.env.NODE_ENV, but in this case, the value entered in the --mode option is injected.

**In my yarn run start case** , process.env.NODE_ENV of React App is `development`.. but I want process.env.NODE_ENV to be `local` .


I'd like to see the value I inject into process.env.NODE_ENV .

**Why is the mode option value injected as process.env.NODE_ENV value?**

**Also, where is the code to inject the mode option value into process.env.NODE_ENV?**

title: Ideas for preventing memory leaks when hash used in `output.filename`
body:
My team was using:

```js
{
  /* ... */
  output: {
    filename: '[name].[chunkhash].js',
    clean: true
  },
  devtool: 'cheap-module-source-map'
}
```

This caused Webpack's memory usage to increase by ~300 MB on each rebuild. I eventually found [this comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12947#issuecomment-804007892) by @sokra:

> Best avoid using contenthashes during development. This does have similar problems in other places, e. g. it writes a lot files into the filesystem (on disk or in memory).

I removed the `output.filename` option, so that the default of `[name].js` is used, and this fixed the rapidly increasing memory usage.

My questions:

1. Since including `[fullhash]` / `[chunkhash]` / `[contenthash]` in `output.filename` is known to cause memory issues, could Webpack print a warning if `output.filename` contains one of these variables and `mode === 'development'`? If yes, then I can attempt a PR.
2. Could Webpack be changed so that it clears cached assets from old builds automatically? I don't know enough about Webpack internals to know if this is feasible.

Ref https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12947

title: Why does WDS recompile immediately after a successful startup
body:
Recently I am learning internal details of Webpack.

For some complex frontend projects, I always encounter a strange case:

After I run webpack-dev-server and it compiles successfully, I will get: `Build completed in XX.XXs`

Immediately, webpack-dev-server starts compiling again and seems to only compile a small number of modules. Soon I get another `Build completed in YY.YYs`

After setting some breakpoints, I notice that the modules(mainly `NormalModule`s in my case) compiled in the secend run were also compiled in the first one, but with different `hash`.

So my questions are:
* Why does webpack-dev-server need a second run of compilation?
* Is it because some modules(or their dependencies) have changed? But how can they be changed during the first run?
* Is it possible to avoid the second run?

I might miss some details, feel free to ask me for them.

Any help will be appreciated, thanks!

Thanks in advance!

title: Lack of javascript error messages with webpack when missing a dependency
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

To get an error message when a dependency is not found at runtime via the webpackBootstrap.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Currently this functionality is not available. The problem is that compiling and bundling works. But once you load your page, the javascript does not execute and it just silently fails. This make its really HARD to determine what is the cause. I have been running around in circles for three days with this, thinking it was an issue with my ts code, ts configuration or webpack confguration.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
I am using the 'extract' option via Laravel MIx, so I get a manifest.js that has the  webpackBootstrap code. I hava emodified the code so it shows an error when a chunk can't be found. This is the relevant section:

```
/* webpack/runtime/chunk loaded */
/******/ 	(() => {
/******/ 		var deferred = [];
/******/ 		__webpack_require__.O = (result, chunkIds, fn, priority) => {
/******/ 			if(chunkIds) {
/******/ 				priority = priority || 0;
/******/ 				for(var i = deferred.length; i > 0 && deferred[i - 1][2] > priority; i--) deferred[i] = deferred[i - 1];
/******/ 				deferred[i] = [chunkIds, fn, priority];
/******/ 				return;
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			var notFulfilled = Infinity;
/******/ 			for (var i = 0; i < deferred.length; i++) {
/******/ 				var [chunkIds, fn, priority] = deferred[i];
/******/ 				var fulfilled = true;
/******/ 				for (var j = 0; j < chunkIds.length; j++) {
                                                  // This breaks the  Object.keys(__webpack_require__.O).every((key) => (__webpack_require__.O[key](chunkIds[j])))
                                                 // Statement into a loop, so when a chunk is misisng we can issue the error.
							var everyChunk = true;
							const keys = Object.keys(__webpack_require__.O);
							keys.forEach((key, index) => {
								if (!__webpack_require__.O[key](chunkIds[j])) {
									console.error(`Unable to find required file: ${chunkIds[j]}. Make sure the relevant file has been added via <script> or <style> element.`)
									everyChunk = false;
								}
							});
/******/ 					if ((priority & 1 === 0 || notFulfilled >= priority) && everyChunk) {
/******/ 						chunkIds.splice(j--, 1);
/******/ 					} else {
/******/ 						fulfilled = false;
/******/ 						if(priority < notFulfilled) notFulfilled = priority;
/******/ 					}
/******/ 				}
/******/ 				if(fulfilled) {
/******/ 					deferred.splice(i--, 1)
/******/ 					var r = fn();
/******/ 					if (r !== undefined) result = r;
/******/ 				}
/******/ 			}
/******/ 			return result;
/******/ 		};
```

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**

yes. But I am not 100% sure I completely understand the webpackBootstrap code to assert if the modification is correct.

title: 'X' is not defined inside Electron
body:
Hi, we are building an Electron app and bumping its dependencies from:

**Webpack:** 4.6 ➡️ 5.7
**Electron:** 14 ➡️ 20
**Angular:** 11 ➡️ 14 (hence the webpack bump)

We adjusted our webpack config and run into issues like these:

```
Uncaught ReferenceError: fs is not defined
...
Uncaught ReferenceError: child_process is not defined
...
Uncaught ReferenceError: worker_threads is not defined
...
```

Our app is a pure offline app and therefore is very loose with security settings (nodeIntegration true, webSecurity off, etc.). Basically, we use the `fs` module within the renderer.

Our `webpack.js` from Webpack 4 has mainly these settings:

```javascript
  target: 'electron-renderer',
  node: {
    fs: 'empty',
  },
  externals: {
    'drivelist': 'require("drivelist")',
    'fsevents': "require('fsevents')",
    'electron-clipboard-ex': "require('electron-clipboard-ex')",
  },
```

Question 1: Electron has a module `fs` that can be included in the renderer. Why did I have to set `fs` to `"empty"` to fix the `'fs' is not defined` error that we are having again with Webpack 5?

Question 2: How can we fix the issue now with Webpack 5? I tried many configurations like adding `fs` to `externals` or using:

```javascript
resolve: {
  fallback: {
    fs: false,
    worker_threads: false
  }
}
```

Thanks a lot for your help!

title: Allow Dynamic Import in `commonjs2`
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
webpack should allow `module` dependencies to be declared using the `externals` setting.

Furthermore, dynamic `import()` calls should be preserved.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
As described in `Node.js`' page, `import()` calls are allowed in `CommonJS` files:
https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#import-expressions

Despite this fact, `webpack` replaces all `import()` calls with `require`s.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
When targeting `commonjs2`, dynamic `import()` calls importing an external `module` should stay untouched. 

Furthermore, adding a `module` external should not report an error even if the target library type is `commonjs2`

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
no

title: What is the difference between 'flag' and true of optimization.sideEffects
body:
```js
/**
 * Skip over modules which contain no side effects when exports are not used (false: disabled, 'flag': only use manually placed side effects flag, true: also analyse source code for side effects).
 */
sideEffects?: "flag" | boolean;
```
i don't understand "flag" and true，In my opinion, these two are the same。

title: webpack build warning: export default was not found
body:
**What is the current behavior?**

I have a project using backbone. When I built the project in my linux server, everything was ok. But when I built it in a docker container, then something went wrong. All node version and npm version are same in two environments, and the operating system are similar too. Here is a very simple example: [webpack-build-demo](https://github.com/ohmyvander/webpack-build-demo)
Even if I change to use webpack 5, the error also occurs.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

After debugging, I found that if the name of project directory is `src`, then webpack cannot bundle amd or cmd module correctly.

title: Random chunk loading errors in production
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I am using vue-cli, which uses Webpack in the background to build a vue3 library with multiple entries.
Shared vendor modules are output into separate chunks.
This works great in development, but I get errors related to dynamic chunk loading in random production builds.
I sometimes end up having to run the build process several times (without changing anything) before getting a working one.
I can't quite figure out what's causing this, and have tried moving to a single entry setup, but this didn't change anything.

The errors are also not always the same, and occur even though the chunk seems to be correctly loaded.
Here are some examples of the errors that occur:

- `Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: e[r] is undefined`
- `ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk 'xx' failed` (although I do see the chunk loading in the browser's network console)

I noticed that **when the errors occur vendor chunks seem to not be minified**, unlike in working builds.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Since the errors occur on random builds, I can't quite create a minimal reproduction example.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

I expect production builds to be consistent and to have dynamic chunks not produce errors when loaded.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.74.0
Node.js version: 16.17.0
Operating System: Debian 10 under WSL
Additional tools: [vue-cli](https://cli.vuejs.org/), [CKEditorWebpackPlugin](https://github.com/ckeditor/ckeditor5-dev)

Such cases seem to have been experienced by others using webpack (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61090881/webpack-laravel-mix-chunk-error-typeerror-er-is-undefined, https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53704950/webpack-code-splitting-loading-chunk-failed-error-wrong-file-path), but are usually due to network/caching issues, which is not my case as I disabled caching in my browser all together and see the chunks loading fine in the network console.

This might be an issue in the Terser plugin, but I don't see any warning or errors when building.
Is there maybe a way to monitor the build process in a more verbose way that what is output by default ?

title: SyntaxError: Unexpected token '.' when bundling a css file
body:
I need help with an external css file from a library. My webpack config for css files is thus (no plugins for css):
```
{
      test: /\.css$/i,
      use: ["style-loader", "css-loader"],
},
```
In my .jsx file I am importing css as follows:
`import 'remirror/styles/all.css';`
When I start webpack, everything at first seems fine:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/67240021/195598273-6953ba3a-1c6c-4225-82bf-9e37e08ece35.png)
But then suddenly there is that disturbing error:
```
C:\Users\User\desktop\projects\chat-app\node_modules\remirror\styles\all.css:4
.remirror-editor-wrapper {
^

SyntaxError: Unexpected token '.'
    at compileFunction (<anonymous>)
    at Object.compileFunction (node:vm:352:18)
    at wrapSafe (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1026:15)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1061:27)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1151:10)
    at Module.load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:975:32)
    at Function.Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:822:12)
    at Module.require (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:999:19)
    at require (node:internal/modules/cjs/helpers:102:18)
    at Object.remirror/styles/all.css (C:\Users\User\desktop\projects\chat-app\dist\server.generated.js:645:18)

Node.js v17.5.0

```
Screenshot:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/67240021/195598450-84ba4d1c-ea93-4ac5-bd04-6bbaa7578d91.png)
To solve the issue, I also tried by adding the following config (instead of the above):
```
{
                test: /\.css$/i,
                oneOf: [
                  {
                    include: /node_modules/,
                    use: [
                      "style-loader",
                      { loader: "css-loader", options: { modules: true } }
                    ]
                  },
                  {
                    use: [
                      "style-loader",
                      "css-loader"
                    ]
                  }
                ]
},
```
But it didn't help! Could someone please suggest me a solution?

title: Confusing module import/export behavior
body:
Hello all,

I am building a little SPA with React + Material UI. I have installed the default material packages but things have gone awry when importing components.

### What is the current behavior?
Running the webpack dev server with `npm start` -> `webpack serve --hot --open` produces the following warning:
```
WARNING in ./node_modules/@mui/material/esm/SvgIcon/SvgIcon.js 24:31-53
export 'getSvgIconUtilityClass' (imported as 'getSvgIconUtilityClass') was not found in './svgIconClasses' (possible exports: default)
```

### What is the expected behavior?
I can browse the subject files and (given they are only a few lines long) can clearly see a named export (`getSvgIconUtilityClass`) alongside a default export. However, I am interpreting the above error to explicitly contradict this.

./node_modules/@mui/material/esm/SvgIcon/svgIconClasses.js:
```js
import { generateUtilityClass, generateUtilityClasses } from '@mui/base';
export function getSvgIconUtilityClass(slot) {
  return generateUtilityClass('MuiSvgIcon', slot);
}
const svgIconClasses = generateUtilityClasses('MuiSvgIcon', ['root', 'colorPrimary', 'colorSecondary', 'colorAction', 'colorError', 'colorDisabled', 'fontSizeInherit', 'fontSizeSmall', 'fontSizeMedium', 'fontSizeLarge']);
export default svgIconClasses;
```

I have actually used the same mui version in a create-react-app before with no issue.. I suspect there is some configuration that was hidden by CRA that I am unable to find (because the ejected configs are overwhelming to a noob like me).

Any suggestions are appreciated. Thanks in advance!

### Other relevant information:
- webpack version: ^5.74.0
- webpack-cli: ^4.10.0
- webpack-dev-server: ^4.11.1
- @mui/material: ^5.10.8
- Node.js version: v16.16.0
- Operating System: Ubuntu 20.04 LTS

```
class AwesomePlugin {
  constructor() {
    this.childCompilerName = 'awesome-plugin-compilation';
    this.outputFileName = 'custom-file.js';
    this.compilationEntry = './src/Hello.js';
  }
  apply(compiler) {
    compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('AwesomePlugin', (compilation, callback) => {
      const childCompiler = compilation.createChildCompiler(this.childCompilerName, {
        filename: this.outputFileName
      });
      childCompiler.options.output.chunkFilename = `child-${compiler.options.output.chunkFilename}`;
      childCompiler.context = compiler.context;
      childCompiler.apply(new SingleEntryPlugin(compiler.context, this.compilationEntry, this.outputFileName));
      childCompiler.plugin('compilation', (compilation) => {
        if (compilation.cache) {
        }
      });
      childCompiler.runAsChild((err, entries, childCompilation) => {
        callback(err);
      });
    });
  }
}
```

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

`__WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE__Hello_js__` this value is undefined.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

This is my webpack config.
```
const path = require('path');
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require('html-webpack-plugin');
const CleanWebpackPlugin = require('clean-webpack-plugin');
const SingleEntryPlugin = require('webpack/lib/SingleEntryPlugin');
const LibraryTemplatePlugin = require('webpack/lib/LibraryTemplatePlugin');

const outputDirectory = 'dist';

class AwesomePlugin {
  constructor() {
    this.childCompilerName = 'awesome-plugin-compilation';
    this.outputFileName = 'custom-file.js';
  }

  apply(compiler) {
    compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('AwesomePlugin', (compilation, callback) => {
      this.compilationEntry = './src/client/Hello.js'
      const childCompiler = compilation.createChildCompiler(this.childCompilerName, {
        filename: this.outputFileName
      }, [
        new SingleEntryPlugin(compiler.context, this.compilationEntry, this.outputFileName),
        new LibraryTemplatePlugin('childLibrary', 'umd')
      ]);
      childCompiler.options.output.chunkFilename = `child-${compiler.options.output.chunkFilename}`; 
      childCompiler.context = compiler.context;
      childCompiler.runAsChild((err, entries, childCompilation) => {
        callback(err);
      });
    });
  }
}

module.exports = {
  entry: ['babel-polyfill', './src/client/index.js'],
  output: {
    path: path.join(__dirname, outputDirectory),
    filename: 'bundle.js',
    chunkFilename: '[name].js',
    library: 'MyLibrary',
    libraryTarget: 'umd',
    //libraryExport: 'default',
  },
  externals: {
    "./Hello.js": 'childLibrary',
  },
  plugins: [
    new CleanWebpackPlugin([outputDirectory]),
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      template: './public/index.html',
      favicon: './public/favicon.ico'
    }),
    new AwesomePlugin(),
  ]
};
```

I have named the parent compiler's library as `MyLibrary` and child compiler's library as `childLibrary`. Also importing the child compiler's bundle as script.

Bundle.js
```
/***/ "./Hello.js":

eval("module.exports = __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE__Hello_js__;\n\n//# sourceURL=webpack://MyLibrary/external_%22childLibrary%22?");

title: v5.75.0
body:
# Bugfixes * `experiments.*` normalize to `false` when opt-out * avoid `NaN%` * show the correct error when using a conflicting chunk name in code * HMR code tests existance of `window` before trying to access it * fix `eval-nosources-*` actually exclude sources * fix race condition where no module is returned from processing module * fix position of standalong semicolon in runtime code # Features * add support for `@import` to extenal CSS when using experimental CSS in node * add `i64` support to the deprecated WASM implementation # Developer Experience * expose `EnableWasmLoadingPlugin` * add more typings * generate getters instead of readonly properties in typings to allow overriding them
This discussion was created from the release v5.75.0.

title: style-loader as part of an npm package?
body:
Hi there 👋, possibly a bit of a rookie question. 

I'm wondering if it's possible for an NPM package to depend on `style-loader` without the consuming application also having to configure it? 

I'm hoping to use `lazyStyleTag` as part of a package in order to lazy-load CSS files at runtime. 
My assumption is that webpack is responsible for building the package and ensuring that the assets that are imported are part of the `dist`, however, does it also inject some sort of runtime into the package to give it the ability to inject the CSS files? 

I'm currently on `webpack@4.41.3` and `style-loader@1.0.0`. 

Here's an example: 

```jsx
// my-package
const myStyles = require('./my-css-file.css');

export const mountCSS = () => {
  myStyles.use();
}
```

Then in my app: 

```jsx
import { mountCSS } from 'my-package';

mountCSS(); // 🎉
```

I receive the following error: 

```
VM317:1 Uncaught ReferenceError: require is not defined
    at eval (eval at ./my-package/dist/esm/mount-css.js
    at options.factory (react refresh:6:1)
    at __webpack_require__ (bootstrap:24:1)
    at fn (hot module replacement:62:1)
    at ./src/App.js (bundle.js:20:74)
    at options.factory (react refresh:6:1)
    at __webpack_require__ (bootstrap:24:1)
    at fn (hot module replacement:62:1)
    at ./src/index.js (App.js:219:1)
```

Have I completely misinterpreted how webpack should work, or have I just done something wrong in my setup?

If I build up my project in development mode - everything is perfect, no any errors. But in production mode I have some weird problem. Sometimes after build up I see an error like this in the Developer Console: As you see, the d8498 is not defined. I have looked into the file 286_6660b.js and saw this: ``` /* harmony import */ var _code_editor__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_5__ = __webpack_require__(d8498); ``` The name of module just not in enclosed quotes, because `d8498` is not valid name for this situation. ## webpack.dev.conf.js
```js
'use strict'

const fs = require('fs')
const path = require('path')
const utils = require('./utils')
const webpack = require('webpack')
const config = require('../config')
const {merge} = require('webpack-merge')
const baseWebpackConfig = require('./webpack.base.conf')
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require('html-webpack-plugin')
const FriendlyErrorsWebpackPlugin = require('@soda/friendly-errors-webpack-plugin')

// add hot-reload related code to entry chunks
Object.keys(baseWebpackConfig.entry).forEach(function (name) {
  baseWebpackConfig.entry[name] = ['./build/dev-client'].concat(baseWebpackConfig.entry[name])
})

// Todo: Remove
process.traceDeprecation = true

module.exports = merge(baseWebpackConfig, {
  mode: 'development',
  module: {
    rules: utils.styleLoaders({ sourceMap: config.dev.cssSourceMap })
  },
  // cheap-module-eval-source-map is faster for development
  devtool: 'cheap-module-source-map',
  plugins: [
    new webpack.DefinePlugin({
      'process.env': config.dev.env
    }),
    // https://github.com/glenjamin/webpack-hot-middleware#installation--usage
    new webpack.HotModuleReplacementPlugin(),
    new webpack.NoEmitOnErrorsPlugin(),
    // https://github.com/ampedandwired/html-webpack-plugin
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin(
      {
      filename: 'index.html',
      template: 'index.dev.html',
      inject: true,
      serviceWorkerLoader: `<script>${fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname,
        './service-worker-dev.js'), 'utf-8')}</script>`
      }
    ),
    new FriendlyErrorsWebpackPlugin(),
  ]
})

'use strict'

const webpack = require('webpack')
const path = require('path')

const config = require('../config')

const VueLoaderPlugin = require('vue-loader/lib/plugin')
const ESLintPlugin = require('eslint-webpack-plugin');
const NodePolyfillPlugin = require('node-polyfill-webpack-plugin');
const MiniCssExtractPlugin = require("mini-css-extract-plugin");

const vueLoaderConfig = require('./vue-loader.conf')

function resolve (dir) {
  return path.join(__dirname, '..', dir)
}

module.exports = {
  plugins: [
    new webpack.ProvidePlugin({
      $: 'jquery',
      jquery: 'jquery',
      'window.jQuery': 'jquery',
      jQuery: 'jquery',
      _: 'lodash'
    }),
    new VueLoaderPlugin(),
    new ESLintPlugin(),
    new NodePolyfillPlugin(),
    new MiniCssExtractPlugin(),
  ],
  entry: {
    app: './src/main.js'
  },
  output: {
    path: config.build.assetsRoot,
    filename: '[name].js',
    publicPath: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'
      ? config.build.assetsPublicPath

  },
  resolve: {
    extensions: ['.js', '.vue', '.json'],

```js

import Es6Promise from 'es6-promise'

/*
 * Vue Core Imports
 */
import Vue from 'vue'
import Vuex from 'vuex'
import App from './App'

import router from './router'
import store from './store'

/*
 * Vue-Resource Import
 * https://github.com/pagekit/vue-resource
 * Note: We opted for Vue-Resource instead of Axios because of an issue with Vue-Auth
 */

title: How to inject a file into a bundle within a custom plugin?
body:
I'm working on adding Webpack support for a popular APM product. One of the requirements is that the APM knows the versions of instrumented libraries (redis, pg, mysql) at runtime. Usually this means calling some semblance of `require('db/package.json')`. This would be fine if the package itself requires its own `package.json` file as it would be added to the bundle automatically. But most packages don't do this.

What I'm trying to do is add the appropriate package.json file to the bundle at runtime. Based on the method name, `complication.addModule()` sounds promising, but after reading documentation and source code I'm still at a loss. I'm hoping to do something like this:

```javascript
const contents = require('redis/package.json'); // should the contents be read?

// should I instantiate a new webpack.Module? sounds correct but can't find it documented anywhere
const packageJson = new webpack.Module('what is module type? appears undocumented');

packageJson.something(); // are there methods to set file contents? or path to file which gets read?

compilation.addModule(packageJson); // it seems that addModule expects a Module instance
```

**Update**: I realized I didn't mention this in the post but I'm specifically trying to create a Webpack plugin, not modify an application.

author: imajim

title: Is there a difference between sideEffects being undefined vs. being set to true?
body:
My assumption was that not explicitly specifying a boolean value for `sideEffects` (IE `undefined`) would be equivalent to setting `"sideEffects": true`, but [this section](https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#inner-module-tree-shaking) seems to imply that even with `sideEffects` being undefined there is some automatic omission of unneeded code.

I guess my specific question is: if we start explicitly setting `"sideEffects": true`, will we be losing any automatic-optimization that is happening when we leave it undefined?

title: Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'SOME_VARIABLE')
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
An error sometimes happens with an import path. It depends on a reexport variable with another code influence. The error looks like:
```
TypeError
Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS')
```

The same issue https://github.com/apollographql/apollo-client/issues/9659 doesn't solve the problem by solution:
```
"@apollo/client": "3.6.2",
"graphql": "15.8.0",
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
I don't know exactly way to reproduce it. Some reexport combinations do it or not. It disappears if the import path changes. It's really unpredictable error in the app code.

**With error** (It is reexport through index.ts: `export { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from './fetchStorefrontSections.gql'`):

```js
import { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from 'src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections'
```

**Without error** (It is direct path):

```js
import { FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS } from 'src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql'
```


I found out, that error throws from generated bundle.js code:

```js
......
/***/ "./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/index.ts":
  /*!***********************************************************************!*\
    !*** ./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/index.ts ***!
    \***********************************************************************/
  /***/ (function(module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) {
  
  "use strict";
  __webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
  /* harmony export */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {
  /* harmony export */   "FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS": function() { return /* reexport safe */ _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS; },
  /* harmony export */ });
  /* harmony import */ var _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__(/*! ./fetchStorefrontSections.gql */ "./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql.ts");
  __webpack_require__.$Refresh$.runtime = __webpack_require__(/*! ./node_modules/react-refresh/runtime.js */ "./node_modules/react-refresh/runtime.js");
......
```

If the `_fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__` variable declaration and the assignment `FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS` property was swapped, the error would be disappear:

```js
var _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__ = __webpack_require__("./src/entities/Storefront/api/fetchStorefrontSections/fetchStorefrontSections.gql.ts");

__webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, {
  "FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS": function() { return _fetchStorefrontSections_gql__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.FETCH_STOREFRONT_SECTIONS; },
});
```

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Anyone of combination reexport paths shouldn't throw error in the runtime.


<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0 or 5.73.0.
Node.js version: 18.12.0 or 16.12.0
Operating System: osx or linux
Additional tools: graphql-tag

title: Sourcemap URL incorrect when bundling webpack
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
When attempting to find source maps, console shows

> "DevTools failed to load source map: Could not load content for http://localhost:3000/assets/bundle.js-7f6807a0f15667890c1616b3417f8d5e6706c2c168dadbd124f0c1de47d079ba.map: HTTP error: status code 404, net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE"

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
In webpack.config.js, include     
`devtool: "source-map"`
Run `webpack --watch --mode=development`.

Upon inspecting bundle.js, the issue is that the `sourceMappingURL` is not pointing to the directory. It shows 
`//# sourceMappingURL=bundle.js.map`, while it should be showing
`//# sourceMappingURL=./bundle.js.map`

Manually changing the URL makes the source maps work, but if I rebundle, it will revert back to the error.

**What is the expected behavior?**
The sourceMappingURL is correct and the source maps load within the Chrome console.

title: How to export multiple entries as an object?
body:
I would like to export multiple entries as an object.
Example:

a.js
b.js
=>
{
 a: { ... },
 b: { ... },
}

I tried using output.libraryTarget = 'var', but it only exported the last entry point.
After a lot of time trying to find information on this, I found the below:

> When combining with the [output.library](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputlibrary) option: If an array is passed only the last item is exported.

https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#entry

This doesn't provide any help on what to do if you want to handle all the entry points.
What is the correct way of handling this?

Regards.
author: goldingdamien

title: Error: EBUSY: resource busy or locked, lstat 'C:\DumpStack.log.tmp'
body:
Hi,

** Just to clarify this is regarding webpack-dev-server, posting here as it doesn't have it's own discussion area

I recently took the time to upgrade to the latest node version and bring all the packages up to their latest versions for a react project I have. Everything seemed fine but every time I ran the dev version after, at most, 20 minutes it would crash with the error in the title. It's been a couple of weeks and I've finally found that it was due to me incorrectly setting the devServer static value to '/public'. I realise this was a case of RTFM and it should have been set to { directory: path.join(__dirname, 'public') } or left out as it'll default to that anyway but given how abstract the error is I was looking to see if there was a way to catch these kind of errors.

I'm not super familiar with the code base but I'm looking at the watchFiles function in lib/Server.js and I'm wondering is there any reason not to add a check to see if the watchPath exists before executing chokidar.watch(watchPath, watchOptions). As far as I understand chokidar will throw this error when you try to watch a path that doesn't exist and I think it would be better throwing a more explicit error during the start up of webpack-dev-server rather than having it throw a fairly vague one at some random point 20 minutes afterwards.

Happy to take advice from more knowledgeable heads before I start hacking a PR.

I am seeing a possible race condition in the chunk loading code of webpack 5. When chunks are loaded from disk cache, I get an error `Loading chunk failed (missing)`. If I disable disk cache, the chunks load perfectly fine. I am using version 5.74.0 of Webpack and the latest version of Chrome and Firefox. You can reproduce using site converse.com.mx. I also have a video showing the reproduction but it is too large to drop in Github.

I think there is a race condition happening between the script load event handler and the jsonp function call.

title: How to use Webpack caching when config file changes frequently
body:
We all know that when the configuration file for webpack changes, the entire file system caching gets invalidated.

In our case, the configuration file needs to change very frequently, essentially each change in source code would imply a change in config file, so does this mean we can't benefit from file system caching at all?

Specifically, the changing config only affects one specific loader (babel-loader). Would be curious to know if we can configure it so that we can still cache & speed up other loaders prior to babel-loader (like ts-loader) using the file system caching?

title: Are there any examples/docs of using the Cache plugin of Webpack 5?
body:
I am trying to use cache when running the development server, so far all the docs have suggested just adding `cache: true` to webpack config. However, this has not worked so far as on initial load it correctly bundles everything, however, on subsequent runs of the development server the assets are not correctly loaded from the cache. We have a plugin to create a dependency graph of the assets and load it alongside and pass it down to a Child compiler, however, when cache is enabled the assets are not correctly passed to the Child compiler.

I would like to create our own CachePlugin to resolve this issue, but there aren't any good docs related to it. Are there any examples of how to use the Cache plugin from webpack 5 to correctly cache the assets we have in a child compiler?

The only post I can see related to this is: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14898, however, not having any luck with the example posted there.

title: usedExports optimization doesn't work when devtool is set to eval-source-map
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Asked a question on SO and later found the answer myself:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/75077181/cant-get-webpack-usedexport-treeshaking-to-work/75087281#75087281

**What is the current behavior?**
When `devtool` is set to `eval-source-map`, `usedExports` settings has no effect.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Set `devtool` to `eval-source-map`, set `usedExports` to `true`, observe that the optimization does not happen.
Repro here: https://github.com/dmt0/treeshake-repro

**What is the expected behavior?**
I'm not exactly sure if this is itself a bug. Perhaps setting `devtool` to a certain value makes `webpack` assume that it's not in `production` mode in which case `usedExports` doesn't make sense. In any case it's not very clear from the docs and it took me a day of debugging until I've tried everything and started removing lines one by one from `webpack.config.js`. If this is indeed a desired behavior, perhaps there needs to be a mention somewhere in the docs.

Treeshaking docs (https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/#conclusion) mention all the conditions necessary for it to work. Again `devtool` is not mentioned.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.75.0
Node.js version: 16.14.0
Operating System: macOS
Additional tools: "@babel/core": "7.20.12"

The request './getPrototypeOf' failed to resolve only because it was resolved as fully specified
(probably because the origin is strict EcmaScript Module, 
e. g. a module with javascript mimetype, 
a '*.mjs' file, or a '*.js' file where the package.json contains '"type": "module"').
```

# What is the current behavior? 
Currently, no version has solved this problem， version 5.64.4 - 5.75.0 have this bug

# Provide the steps to reproduce.
just run build it ,then u can see the bug. 
npm run build 
or 
npm run buildR

    ],

    ]

```

# Temporary solution
webpack.config.js
module -> rules -> oneOf 
add this:

author: geektcp

title: Error with sass-loader possibly: "Cannot find module 'sass'" when building to DEV environment
body:
Hi Webpack community, I need help resolving a bug that I cannot solve on own. My team is trying to deploy our website after upgrading to Node v16 and Webpack v5. The problem is our webpack "sass-loader" is erroring out because it claims the "sass" module is not found.

_NOTE: our current config works on our localhost but not when deployed in a DEV environment!_

The error in our logs:
```
uncaughtException: Cannot find module 'sass'
Require stack:
- /var/node/atlas/build/webpack.config.js
- /var/node/atlas/server/main.js
- /var/node/atlas/bin/server.js

at Object.<anonymous> (/var/node/atlas/build/webpack.config.js:119:21)
```

Our webpack config file looks like this. Our setup is exactly what the Webpack documentation recommends:
```
//webpack.config.js
115 const sassLoader = {
116  loader: 'sass-loader',
117  options: {
118    sourceMap: true,
119    implementation: require('sass') /** THIS IS FAILING according to our logs */
120  }
121 };

// how sassLoader is being used
webpackConfig.module.rules.push({
  test: /\.scss$/,
  include: isInClientFolder,
  exclude: isGlobalStyle,
  use: [
    'style-loader',
    cssLoader,
    sassLoader
  ]
});
```

We checked to make sure our **package.json** has all libraries that need to be installed. It seems fine:
```
// package.json
"devDependencies": {
    "sass": "^1.57.1",
    "sass-loader": "^13.0.2",
    "style-loader": "^3.3.1",
    "webpack": "^5.74.0",
    "webpack-dev-middleware": "^5.3.3",
    "webpack-hot-middleware": "^2.25.2"
  }
```


Last of all, we tried installing Sass globally on our **Dockerfile** but still getting the error:
```
// Dockerfile
FROM node:16.19.0

RUN npm install npm@8.19.3 -g

RUN npm config set @dpb-packages:registry=https://dpb-repositories-183626424367.d.codeartifact.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/npm/npm-repository/ && \
    npm config set //dpb-repositories-183626424367.d.codeartifact.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/npm/npm-repository/:_authToken=$CODEARTIFACT_AUTH_TOKEN && \
    npm config set //dpb-repositories-183626424367.d.codeartifact.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/npm/npm-repository/:always-auth=true && \
    npm cache clear --force && \
    npm install && \
    npm install -g sass@1.57.1 && \
    mv node_modules /var/node/atlas
```

Currently this configuration does not work and we are still getting the _"Cannot find module 'sass'"_ error. 

I've already tried:
1. Moving 'sass' in package.json from devDependencies to dependencies but it breaks other loaders in Webpack.
2. I've tried installing Sass globally in Dockerfile but it didn't change anything
3. I've used the default Webpack sass-loader by just using a string 'sass-loader' in the webpack config but also broke the other loaders
4. Tried configuring the sass-loader options to turn off "fibers" which has to do with sync/async loading of the 'sass' package but it didn't do anything. And also Webpack docs say "fibers" doesn't work with Node v16 and up.

Can anyone offer any other ideas on how to fix this?

Thank you!
author: wangytangy

author: hamzahamidi

title: Unable to catch the error thrown by dev Server on port conflict.
body:
We have a wrapper on top of `webpack-dev-server`. 
With webpack 4, when we started dev-server passing a port which was occupied we were getting `EDDRINUSE` logged in the console.
However, with webpack5 we do not see it getting logged & unable to catch the [error thrown](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-server/blob/d1d14340d34ed5e5a97293cee09f49a85428c46f/lib/Server.js#L2512) by the server in our wrapper code.

Any suggestion on how to handle it & catch the error emitted.

Thanks in advance
author: subodhkumar

title: Completely reset webpack config when creating a child compiler
body:
We need inline-JavaScript to initialize advertisements, tracking and some other things. We're also using Next.js, which does not really offer good tools to create inline JavaScript (other than providing code as plain-text).

So we built our own tool, that uses a child compiler to render some parts of our code and inline its results during the SSR process.

This method has a flaw: Since we are doing this on the server, the child compiler uses a webpack config that targets node, rather than the browser. We already overwrote some settings, but not all. This setup is a bit fragile, we have unknown unknowns in regards to which settings we need to overwrite.

It would be nice to be able to use a child compiler but start from a blank config and be able to provide a clean browser-targeted config. Is there any way to do this?
author: LukasBombach

title: NormalModule original source is undefined when cache is enabled
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

When the webpack cache is enabled, the module source inside the compilation is not accessible, it returns _randomly_ `undefined`. The source is get with [`NormalModule.originalSource()._value`](https://github.com/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.ts#L159) in the plugin `svg-chunk-webpack-plugin`.

The plugin uses the `processAssets` hook with the stage `PROCESS_ASSETS_STAGE_ADDITIONAL`. All stages listed in the webpack documentation produces the same error ([list of asset processing stages](https://webpack.js.org/api/compilation-hooks/#list-of-asset-processing-stages)).

The cache is enabled with this code

```js
cache: {
	type: 'filesystem'
}
```

The error log produced by webpack is the following. The error appears when the module source is decoded from JSON.

```txt
[webpack-cli] HookWebpackError: Unexpected token u in JSON at position 0
    at makeWebpackError (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/HookWebpackError.js:48:9)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:3060:12
    at eval (eval at create (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:20:1)
    at processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:96:5)
-- inner error --
SyntaxError: Unexpected token u in JSON at position 0
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.optimizeSvg (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:41:24)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:52
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.processEntry (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:21)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:68
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.addAssets (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:32)
    at fn (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:536:19)
    at _next0 (eval at create (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:12:17)
caused by plugins in Compilation.hooks.processAssets
SyntaxError: Unexpected token u in JSON at position 0
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.optimizeSvg (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:41:24)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:52
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.processEntry (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:120:21)
    at /Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:68
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at SvgSprite.addAssets (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/lib/index.js:97:32)
    at fn (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compilation.js:536:19)
    at _next0 (eval at create (/Users/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:12:17)
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. Clone the code from https://github.com/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/pull/8
2. Execute `nvm use` or switch to Node.js `v12.22.7`
3. Install dependencies with `npm install`
4. Run the example in development mode with `npm run start:example`
5. The webpack cache is created in `node_modules/.cache/webpack`
6. Exit the watcher and re-run it
7. The error appears when the plugin tried to access the module source (svg file)
8. Remove the webpack cache
9. Re-run the watcher
10. It works, because the cache is empty

<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

The module source should be accessible.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: `5.72.0` (already tried with the latest without success)
Node.js version: `12.22.7`
Operating System: `macOS 12.6`

title: After upgrading from webpack v4 to v5, getting "Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (...)"
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** Upgrading from webpack v4 to v5 through upgrading react-scripts from v4 to v5 renders only a blank page while giving the following errors: ``` index.tsx:7 Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'ApiKeyRow') at Module.ApiKeyRow (index.tsx:7:1) at Module.ApiKeyRow (index.tsx:78:1) at registerExportsForReactRefresh (RefreshUtils.js:146:1) at Object.executeRuntime (RefreshUtils.js:177:1) at $ReactRefreshModuleRuntime$ (index.ts:2:1) at ./src/shared/index.ts (index.ts:2:1) at options.factory (react refresh:6:1) at __webpack_require__ (bootstrap:24:1) at fn (hot module replacement:62:1) at ./src/shared/ui/Card/EndpointCard/index.jsx (index.tsx:17:1) ``` Mind you, this is not specific to any react components. If I delete ApiKeyRow component, for example, it will just choose another random component to complain about. It's not something with the component itself, since the error persists even after removing all code and returning only a
test

title: Developing a Plugin for BabylonJS and need ideas to make it work
body:
**TL;DR**
How can I change a typescript module in memory level and return it to get processed by webpack?

----

Hey all, I'm trying to create a plugin for webpack to work longside with BabylonJS. The idea is simple, allow the user to import the model from the current folder, instead of add it globally on a asset folder, just like how we do with SCSS for React for example.

My idea was "simple", for each module of the project, get its content as string, search for calls of `SceneLoader` on it and for each call get the path of it (starting for the current folder of the module). With that path, get the model content, get the textures that the model imports and copy all of them (both model and texture) in the dist folder inside a folder with a hashed name (`dist/modules/[hash]/`). Also I want to repath the `SceneLoader` to that new path on dist, only on build/dev-server, dont changing the actuall file, changing it only on memory level for webpack process.

This is being a HUGE challenge for me cause I've never worked with webpack plugins and everything I tried didn't work, the cloeses I got to what I wanted was using the `compiler.hooks.compilation` and `compilation.hooks.buildModule` filtering for files that ends with `.ts`, but when I try to access the `_source` to get the file content its not exactly the module before it got processed (it has some changes compared to the original file) but eitherway I cannot access the file content because it is `null` leading to a error. 

I also thought to create a webpack loader, but for it I would need to communicate between the loader and plugin (since loader is just to proccess the files) and I wasnt able to make such communication work also.

Its also important to note that I'm not able to `import` the modules with the `import` keyword as the way we import models on BabylonJS is just a plain method such as `SceneLoader.ImportMeshAsync()`.

I have several questions about this, so:
- Is there a way to do what I wanted with webpack?
- What would be the right way to make something like this?
- Am I tryign to solve it in a "dumb way"? What would be a better solution?

title: get rid of `new Function('return this')()` in the final bundle
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

it looks like we still have `new Function('return this')()` in the final bundle even just target for web, i assume its from the global shim: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/b67626c7b4ffed8737d195b27c8cea1e68d58134/lib/runtime/GlobalRuntimeModule.js#L19


![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/121891361/219204465-4aba3af5-e959-49be-b962-57d2aadad275.png)

it will not cause any csp violation in most browsers as the path will not be executed, but when it does, it might hit CSP issues. I just saw this post (https://mathiasbynens.be/notes/globalthis) and it seems that we can get rid of the `new Function` for most browsers (except IE9, 10).

Wondering if it's possible we adapt that and makes an exception just for IE9,10 so if  IE9,10 not included in the `browserslist`, then the final bundle does not need the `new Function('return this')()`

**What is the expected behavior?**

no `new Function('return this')()` if target browser does not include IE9, 10

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

it's weird to see `new Function()` call when work on CSP, would be great if we can live without it.

title: webpack 4 hardcode md4 as hash function which not able to use on node 17 or above
body:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/webpack-4/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L25

Seriously???

```
const hashFilename = name => {
	return crypto
		.createHash("md4")
		.update(name)
		.digest("hex")
		.slice(0, 8);
};
```

You guys provide output.hashFunction option but it is useless.

- I'm using nuxtjs, which only support webpack 4: https://github.com/nuxt/nuxt/issues/9268
- webpack4 hardcode md4 in hash filename
- md4 is forbidden on node 17 and above

PLEASE provide a option for webpack4 to use custom hash function for hashFilename.

Related stackoverflow question (anwsers are useless).
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69692842/error-message-error0308010cdigital-envelope-routinesunsupported

There is [a repo](https://github.com/bug-brain/pnpm-webpack-monaco-bug) with a minimal reproduction and parts of the original and generated code. Long story short: There is something wrong with the order of dependencies; a function of a module is called before it has been fully initialized.

title: [Module Federation] Development server hot reload updates are failing
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

My development server starts fine and serves everything correctly. However, whenever I save a file I get an error message like this and the hot reload doesn't update the file.

```
Uncaught TypeError: Cannot set properties of undefined (setting '../node_modules/ts-loader/index.js??clonedRuleSet-1!../node_modules/vue-loader/dist/index.js??ruleSet[0]!./src/components/my-component.vue?vue&type=script&lang=ts')
    at self.webpackHotUpdateLocalTestBuild (remoteEntry.js:5491:39)
    at src_initialize-app_ts-data_image_png_base64_iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAEAAAACCAIAAAAW4yFwAAAAA-651236.e337b2d8e1999818c118.hot-update.js:10:39
self.webpackHotUpdateLocalTestBuild @ remoteEntry.js:5491
(anonymous) @ src_initialize-app_ts-data_image_png_base64_iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAEAAAACCAIAAAAW4yFwAAAAA-651236.e337b2d8e1999818c118.hot-update.js:10
```

I am unsure if this issue is vue-loader specific, because most updates to regular *.ts files completely refresh the page instead of trying to update in-place.

Interestingly, and importantly: this hot reload error **only occurs** when one of the module federation "remotes" is running. If I shut off the remote, my main shell application complains that it can't reach it when it tries to import it, but then the hot reloads start working again for it!

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Create a standard Vue 3 application with typescript. Implement module federating by referencing the settings in the webpack configuration below.

Unfortunately I cannot share the source application, it is closed source. I am mainly interested if there is some incompatibility in the webpack configuration I am using with module federation that would cause this issue, or perhaps an incompatibility with module federation and the vue-loader. Or maybe plugin order?

title: webpack is not noticing changes to lib delibered via commit id
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
1. Initial state
I am using angular + yarn + a dependecy which is delievered via git commit e.g.
```json
"dependencies": {
    "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash1111",
}
```
then I do a `yarn install` and `ng serve` and all is good. 
2. Make a change in the `myproj` dependency and commit it inside the `myproj` repo. After the commit a build is triggered for this dependency and the `dist` folder is uploaded as a commit in the `https://myproj.git` project (the `dist` folder has essentialy the output of the `ng build` command).
3. Consume the new version `somehash2222` by changing the `package.json` to
```json
"dependencies": {
    "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://someurl.git#somehash222",
}
```
3. Run `yarn install` and `ng serve`. The content of the old `somehas1111` is delivered. To resolve the issue one needs to clean the angular cache (which in turn cleans the `webpack` cache from the `.angular` folder).

Now at that point I know that `node_modules` is considered immutable and to my understanding the new `immutable` version of the `@myorg/myproj` is correctly delivered via a version change `somehash1111` -> `somehash2222`. To permanently workaround that I have used the [managedpaths](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#managedpaths) option to instruct `webpack` to start observing this dir via a regexp similar to the `@asure` example. Which it does and now whenever someone changes the hash delievered dependency it is automatically picked up. But according to my understanding the additional config shall not be needed as there is a clear version change. Can it be that 'webpack' is confused as this is not a semver or because it does not look as dir?

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
See steps 1 to 3 for retproduction

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
when I change the dependency from ` "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash1111"` to ` "@myorg/myproj": "git+https://myproj.git#somehash2222"` would be nice for webpack to notice the change then rebuild and most importantly update the cache.
 
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  (should be 5.75.0)
We are using it from angular 15 like that (snippet from yarn.lock)
```json
"@angular-devkit/build-angular@npm:15.2.1":
  version: 15.2.1
  resolution: "@angular-devkit/build-angular@npm:15.2.1"
  dependencies:
    "@ampproject/remapping": 2.2.0
    "@angular-devkit/architect": 0.1502.1
    "@angular-devkit/build-webpack": 0.1502.1
    "@angular-devkit/core": 15.2.1
    "@babel/core": 7.20.12
    "@babel/generator": 7.20.14
    "@babel/helper-annotate-as-pure": 7.18.6

    "@babel/plugin-proposal-async-generator-functions": 7.20.7
    "@babel/plugin-transform-async-to-generator": 7.20.7
    "@babel/plugin-transform-runtime": 7.19.6
    "@babel/preset-env": 7.20.2
    "@babel/runtime": 7.20.13
    "@babel/template": 7.20.7
    "@discoveryjs/json-ext": 0.5.7
    "@ngtools/webpack": 15.2.1
    ansi-colors: 4.1.3
    autoprefixer: 10.4.13
    babel-loader: 9.1.2
    babel-plugin-istanbul: 6.1.1
    browserslist: 4.21.5
    cacache: 17.0.4
    chokidar: 3.5.3
    copy-webpack-plugin: 11.0.0

    esbuild-wasm: 0.17.8

    karma-source-map-support: 1.4.0
    less: 4.1.3

    license-webpack-plugin: 4.0.2

    parse5-html-rewriting-stream: 7.0.0

title: Load a web worker with different public path
body:
Hello! I'm currently having difficulty creating a web-worker due to the same-origin restrictions. The assets of my web app are hosted on a CDN, which makes it impossible for me to load the web worker from the default asset public path. I am attempting to use the new syntax for creating a web worker with `new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url))` in Webpack 5, but it seems that when I use module assets rules for the worker, Webpack creates a second separate asset for the worker and doesn't change the path in the `new Worker(...)` statement in source code. Here are my main points:

1. I have a web worker with modules imports in it and it may have been written in TypeScript, although for now I'm just using JavaScript for simplicity.
2. I want the worker to be loaded from the public path that matches my web app's domain, rather than the CDN public path, to bypass same-origin restrictions.
3. I can create a proxy within my web app's server that routes the request to the real CDN, so I want to emit my workers to a specific output path inside my dist folder, such as workers/. It can look something like `https://mywebapp-domain.com/workers/worker1` -> `https://my-cdn.com/path-to-my-build-dist/workers/worker1`.

Is it possible to achieve this with the new Worker(new URL('./worker.js', import.meta.url)) syntax? I have created a simple reproduction case in this Github repository: https://github.com/gretzkiy/webpack-url-assets-test/tree/master/project. Thank you for your help!
author: gretzkiy

title: Normalize newlines to `\n` in source maps
body:
(Disclaimer: I am not very familiar with JavaScript development and webpack, so any additional recommendations are appreciated.)

I would like to build a release artifact with webpack and include source maps inside it. However, the issue I am facing is that the source maps seem to preserve the original newlines from the source files, that is on Windows it might be `\r\n` (especially with default Git settings) whereas on Linux it will be `\n`. This prevents the release artifact from being reproducible because the source maps and therefore their checksums might differ depending on which OS you are build on.

Is there some way to configure webpack to normalize newlines in source maps to `\n`?

A workaround for this might be to use a `.gitattributes` file to force usage of `\n` regardless of OS and global Git settings, see for example the [documentation on GitHub](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/getting-started-with-git/configuring-git-to-handle-line-endings#per-repository-settings).

title: Caching issue when generating files in the build folder
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
This is a minimal reproduction of the issue we are facing - we are trying to generate files inside the build folder at build time thanks to a custom loader and a custom plugin. However, this has been behaving randomly and the files aren't always moved and populated properly, which means that the different pages can't access the files at runtime. We have managed to narrow it done to the cache as every time we delete the build folder, it would be working. Using `useCache: false` in the webpack config means the issue isn't happening.


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
Minimal reproduction on a NextJS app: https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro 
* Run `yarn` to install the dependencies.
* Make sure to delete the /build folder if you have one.
* Run yarn dev and open "localhost:3000", it should be an empty page with "Hello world".  
* We are expecting the custom plugin (https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro/blob/main/webpack/plugin/test-plugin.js) and the custom loader (https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro/blob/main/webpack/loader/test-loader.js) to have run. In the IDE, open /build/testing/text.txt and make sure it's populated with the string ", one, two, three, four".
* Kill the yarn dev and re-run it. Re-check /build/testing/text.txt - it should be an empty file. /!\ You might need to do this step a couple of times as the issue appears randomly and not necessarily on the second run.


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com/ is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Every time `yarn dev` is running, the file "text.txt" should be created in the `/build/testing` folder and should be populated with the relevant string (in this case, ", one, two, three, four" for example).

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.76.2
Node.js version: 18.15.0
- Operating System: 
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 22.3.0: Mon Jan 30 20:38:37 PST 2023; root:xnu-8792.81.3~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
- Additional tools:
      next: 13.2.4
      eslint-config-next: N/A
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0
      Yarn: 1.22.19

author: janlent1

title: What is the best way to use barrel files and get appropriate tree-shaking/code-splitting?  Nx, Next, Monorepo
body:
Greetings. 

I am currently trying to track down an issue around tree-shaking/code-splitting and have come to the conclusion that it must have something to do with how webpack handles modules. I apologize for how scattered this description may seem, but I supply a repo demonstrating what I'm talking about down below, with instructions about how to replicate.

We use the nx build system and have a next app and then libraries for constants, business logic, ui, etcetera. So our top level folders of concern are apps/ and libs/. Inside the app folder, we implement a pretty straight-ahead next app, bringing in code from our libraries through their top-level barrel files, with aliasing using paths in the tsconfig.base.json

In our libs folder, we have different nx-generated react and javascript libraries. When these generate, they have index.ts barrel files, and we use them. So a barrel file might be

libs/ui/src/index.ts
```typescript
export * from './lib/foo'
export * from './lib//bar'
```

Foo and bar would be subfolders within this library, **with their own barrel file**, exporting different things out of their local folder. Like so:

libs/ui/src/libs/foo/index.ts
```typescript
export * from 'baz'
```
where baz is a **non-default**  exported react component, for example.

Finally, we set tsconfig.base paths around the top-level barrel file, like this:

tsconfig.base.json
```json
{
    "paths": {
        "@company/ui": ["libs/ui/src/index.ts"],
    }
}
```

which allows us to do this:

```typescript
import { baz } from '@company/ui'
```

It works insofar as we get autocomplete/intellisense in vscode, but it may have an unintended consequence when its comes to building the next app. It appears that when you import **anything** from '@company/ui', like a baz, everything from that top-level library barrel file is included in the chunk--even code that never runs.

Does webpack interpret barrel files as "import all of this no matter if it's used or not" ?

This behavior may very well be intended. That's why I'm posting it in a discussion and not an issue, but I would like to better understand how webpack handles this situation.

If all that was confusing, no worries, here's a repo: https://github.com/telestrial/nx-webpack-barrel-issue

Just follow the README instructions to understand what I mean. The assumptions I'm making are there.

Thank you!

EDIT: One final detail regarding my linked repo: This is not just a development problem. I have confirmed that what you see in coverage is what gets included in a production build/bundle/chunks.
author: telestrial

title: Dynamic import of resources/assets with variable path is broken because such imports are always processed as not having a file extension and are being treated as modules
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** It's impossible to perform a dynamic import of a non-JS module (a PNG picture for example) using variable paths.
The next code will not add a file to a bundle, even if ithe file exists: > const name="picture"; > import( `./resources/${name}.png`); The problem is in the current module resolving algorithm. I quote the [documentation](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-resolution/#module-paths): > Once the path is resolved based on the above rule, the resolver checks to see if the path points to a file or a directory. If the path points to a file: > > If the path has a file extension, then the file is bundled straightaway. > Otherwise, the file extension is resolved using the [resolve.extensions](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/resolve/#resolveextensions) option, which tells the resolver which extensions are acceptable for resolution e.g. .js, .jsx. But if an import has a variable path (no matter which kind - even if it's a full path with an insertion of a variable containing an empty string), webpack decides that there is no extension and performs a module resolution, which means that the file must have a valid module extension. So only files with ".js", ".ts" and are included into a bundle. The module resolution performed before any module rules are checked but after include/exclude import rules. **What is the expected behavior?** The documentation states and the commons sense tells it must be possible to import other module types than plain JS-modules. If an extension is being provided in the import path, then it must be taken in account. And a specified extension it seems to be the most popular and common case when a non-module is dynamically imported. If no extension is provided/detected then the imported files must be checked and if there are any non-empty "module"-extensions then the module resolution must be performed. The good question is: What should be done if both JS-modules and other file types are detected? There are two main possible choices: not import non-modules in a module has been found (but it can be tricky to execute if a whole subtree is imported), or just import everything and let the user decide (but I suppose that there can be some side effects with importing complex modules). Because of that, I see as the most simple solution is when, webpack might require to always specify an extension if anything else apart from a module is being imported with dynamic import AND variable path. If a user wants several file types to be imported, they can use multiple import statements or use other ways to force webpack to include the files/ **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.76.3 Node.js version: 18.15.0 Operating System: Linux Additional tools:

title: mangle configuration does not work when zipping on different vms.
body:
Hi, all

I am using a CI pipeline which enables multiple vms as a compile pool to compile js projects.

As we are forced to remove binary differences on different compilation environments, so I turned on the configuration:

`mangle: ['exports']` to prevent my variables from changing when compressed.

But the outcome shows part of variables change if I compile my project on different vms in the pool.

For example:

`...q(n)?{unit:"minute",value:n}:(n=function(e,t){return(t.valueOf()-e.valueOf())/1e3}(e,t)...`

and

`...L(n)?{unit:"minute",value:n}:(n=function(e,t){return(t.valueOf()-e.valueOf())/1e3}(e,t)...`

Any help?

title: Proxy does not work with my PHP backend.
body:
Hi All, I was experimenting today with my webpack devserver config and was trying to get data from my **`php backend running on port 80`**

I make calls in to my php backend like this from my ` react frontend which runs on port 3000`:
`const resp =  await fetch('/api/getXYZ.php');`

My proxy setting in my webpack config looks like this:
```
proxy: {
            '/api': {
                target: 'http://localhost',
                secure: false,
                changeOrigin: true
            },
        },
```

When I open  ` http://localhost/api/getXYZ.php` on my browser it returns me the JSON. But when the react app makes the request I get  this error

` [webpack-dev-server] [HPM] Error occurred while proxying request localhost:3000/api/getXYZ.php to http://localhost/ [ECONNREFUSED]`

I check the network tab and I see the 504 error instead.

So to check if the proxy itself was working or not I changed my code to point to some API on the web which returns a JSON. I made the following changes/including target changes to point to a different server.

**React:**
`const resp =  await fetch('/api/v2/users?size=2&is_xml=true');`

**webpack.config.json:** (Changed target)
```
proxy: {
            '/api': {
                target: 'http://random-data-api.com',
                secure: false,
                changeOrigin: true
            },
        },
```

and this changes seems to work from both browser and react. 

What is special about 'random-data-api.com' compared to 'localhost' ? Spent a few hours on this and not getting anywhere. Please help!!!
