author: chiahao-codes

title: Is there an alternative to maxSize that only divides chunks that are bigger than the maximum size?
body:
We have a quite large legacy code base that we still need to keep around for some time. In this legacy code we define an enormous chunk group, called "main-chunk", that contains a lot of the modules needed for the application to start up.  In total, this chunk group adds up to around 30MB in total, minified.  Yeah.

By default, splitChunks divides this big chunk group up into a reasonably decent set of chunks, with the biggest ones being fairly manageable and no one chunk being too much bigger than the others.  With parallel loading, this works OK.  There are **44** chunks in total for this chunk group and here's a sample of the sizes of the top 10 biggest:

```
9,057,348 bytes     app.chunk.127.b8ed28aa42971788b84b.js
8,140,527 bytes     app.chunk.125.8887a9182690221243e6.js
3,425,898 bytes     app.chunk.587.ceb35d7aaa6a57b48ee4.js
2,684,203 bytes     104.main.812b6f9738f7c5d134c9.js
1,772,276 bytes     app.chunk.109.80f222eb6d9fa889e406.js
1,479,956 bytes     app.chunk.165.572d8fcac8d5aa209d6e.js
1,137,194 bytes     app.chunk.59.410e56b94b429e2b76b5.js
  613,837 bytes     app.chunk.62.cc2647202f1927db86ff.js
  491,345 bytes     app.chunk.113.13eab1895285144b1ee7.js
  486,737 bytes     app.chunk.108.b9698af8335c16bf5503.js
```

Recently we removed a dependency that affected the relationship between quite a number of modules. The code didn't change almost at all -- it was a 1 line change -- but the dependency graph looks quite different.  Now splitChunks produced something a lot less optimal than what is shown above.  The main-chunk group is now split into **42** chunks with the top 10 looking like this:

```
19,232,271 bytes     app.chunk.141.72cf0a6af2b0d67ed7ba.js
 3,463,098 bytes     app.chunk.583.b14b932fa1ae27b79ef0.js
 2,686,691 bytes     101.main.62ef0743278eae180843.js
 1,504,093 bytes     app.chunk.163.873a9077051541824962.js
 1,137,194 bytes     app.chunk.59.e69c0f1627d4dc12b514.js
   613,846 bytes     app.chunk.67.6e8fa43a7603e4706375.js
   581,792 bytes     app.chunk.121.8c475b26cee87d68ed52.js
   485,806 bytes     app.chunk.138.131cd6536da10c66015f.js
   387,907 bytes     app.chunk.110.7ea583702b78563b384d.js
   297,469 bytes     app.chunk.89.872c589c26595580f546.js
```

That giant 19MB chunk causes a performance problem because all chunks from this group need to be loaded before execution can continue.  And that one chunk, being so much bigger than the others, causes a lag waiting for it to complete.

So we want to go back to something like we had before, where the biggest chunk is around 9MB and -- more importantly -- the very largest chunks are roughly the same size.

From the documentation, it looks like [maxSize](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunksmaxsize) is the splitChunks setting we want.  But I can't get it to work the way I want.  I would like to it to do nothing more than to just take the one chunk that is too big, and split that chunk into 2 roughly equal sized parts.  But maxSize seems to do a lot more than that.

After playing around to find the right size (because maxSize works based on the size of non-minified modules) I landed on `maxSize: 25 * 1024 * 1024` being the setting that would bring the biggest chunk down to what it was before.  But the problem is with the side effects.  With that setting in place, splitChunks now produces **50** chunks (instead of 44 like before), with the top 10 looking like this:

```
8,680,103 bytes     app.chunk.141.5cf2713f15cd173ec442.js
4,874,124 bytes     app.chunk.142.b01fd16dc2c95ba0ea19.js
3,463,242 bytes     app.chunk.591.0c15bf73fa34fbd8ec62.js
2,686,691 bytes     101.main.03d5f894d0b20bc86c57.js
2,625,137 bytes     app.chunk.145.ad0ee4ade81d8037d5ad.js
1,504,093 bytes     app.chunk.171.66029ab9a1192993f545.js
1,137,194 bytes     app.chunk.59.e69c0f1627d4dc12b514.js
1,032,509 bytes     app.chunk.147.c8787956ffce1c85d2a7.js
  716,825 bytes     app.chunk.150.8e77383272d259dae531.js
  613,846 bytes     app.chunk.67.49307aa0b189cdefa45a.js
```
You can see that it doesn't just split the one chunk that exceeds the maxSize threshold, but instead it juggles a whole bunch of other stuff around, resulting in more modules than before and a different size distribution from top to bottom.  The fact that there are now 50 modules instead of 44 negates any performance benefit that we see from reducing the size of the largest chunk.  So we're no better off.

What I'm really looking for is just is a simple setting that does nothing else but take a chunk that exceeds a maximum size and divide that chunk into 2 (or more, as necessary) chunks of roughly equal size.  It should not touch any other chunk.

This is not what maxSize does.  Is there another plugin I can use for this?  Or is there another configuration option that might behave like this?

I'm open to any ideas you might have.  If you're still reading this far, thank you in advance.

title: Ignore/exclude an entrypoint or configuration from WDS HMR handling?
body:
We are building up our new docs site with Docusaurus for our TypeScript game engine library. The library itself is packaged up with Webpack as both a UMD module and native ES modules (two separate builds).

Since we are using Docusaurus, that also uses Webpack and serves the local docs under webpack-dev-server.

The use case I'm trying to accomplish is to ensure that any live playgrounds (StackBlitz) are running against the _local changes_ to our library to smooth out the developer experience. That way, if you are making a change locally, and you adjust an API or add a new feature, you can write the docs and see your local changes reflected in all the playground examples. The alternative is that the playgrounds have to reference stable or tagged npm packages which won't reflect any local changes, causing a two-phase kind of workflow.

So what I'm trying to do with the Docusaurus webpack config is build a _local_ production copy (or dev, if I can make it work) of the library while running the docs so that we can pass the local build `.js` file to a sandbox playground (StackBlitz).

The way I've tried to accomplish this is through a separate entrypoint as well as a MultiCompiler config. Both approaches can work during a _build_ but not under WDS. It's very similar to [this issue](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-server/issues/4469).

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/563819/d2c63658-ad75-4a74-b30c-7bdf7b24f0b2)

The issue is that during development, the library `.js` file includes all the WDS/HMR runtime stuff. ~If I understand correctly, that doesn't allow the `.js` file to be a standalone bundle I can just include on a page or reference in an import (outside of Webpack, in Stackblitz). It's instead meant to be used/loaded by the runtime, which isn't what I want when I send the contents of the file over to Stackblitz.~

**Update 11/21:** I actually discovered that the WDS build _does_ work standalone in Stackblitz, but [only if you fork the project](https://github.com/stackblitz/core/issues/2850) in the embedded version. This leads me to believe it's actually a Stackblitz compiler issue, not WDS. So my original question is "answered" (can WDS bundles run standalone? yes) but also leads to a new question:

**Is there a way to disable WDS/HMR handling of a configuration or entrypoint?**

In effect, **I want a local standalone production-ready build of the library** alongside the WDS docs pipeline without having to run two separate commands/processes.

Docusaurus gives us a hook to modify/merge the webpack config; I had to manually patch in MultiCompiler support to test that but I also got that working as an option.

Here is my current config: [docusaurus.config.ts](https://github.com/excaliburjs/Excalibur/blob/67d8c61aa5778371e2cdd92d694d775686eb6fe7/site/docusaurus.config.ts#L106)

I want to believe this is possible but there's probably some special sauce I need to get it working.

Hoping some webpack wizards may be able to point me in the right direction. I felt like it was close with that linked issue, somehow removing the HMR middleware and only applying it to the app entrypoint, but I got lost with how that works.
author: kamranayub

title: Critical dependency: require function is used in a way in which dependencies cannot be statically extracted
body:
A number of developers have recently been getting the above warning from `webpack` due to a `supabase` -> `websockets` -> `node-gyp-build` dependency

The code in `node-pre-gyp` that's raising the warning is a conditional require statement:
```
if (typeof require.addon === 'function') { // if the platform supports native resolving prefer that
  module.exports = require.addon.bind(require)
} else { // else use the runtime version here
  module.exports = require('./node-gyp-build.js')
}
```

The issue has been discussed here: https://github.com/prebuild/node-gyp-build/issues/62
And here: https://github.com/supabase/realtime-js/issues/265#issuecomment-1820546931

The `node-gyp-build` team have an internal javascript runtime that requires the conditional.

The question I have is: is it a requirement of webpack to not use conditionals around require statements?

Here's a link to the warning: in `webpack`:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/87660921808566ef3b8796f8df61bd79fc026108/lib/dependencies/CommonJsImportsParserPlugin.js#L216

I looked for the code where that configuration flag is set, but was not able to locate, so it wasn't clear based on the code. Please let us know. Thanks!

@kasperisager @mafintosh @filipecabaco

Additionally, I have developed a simple Microfrontend (MF) application that directly utilizes Webpack for its setup. ### Issue I'm facing an issue related to Module Federation in this setup. The problem occurs when I try to share modules between my CRA application (configured with CRACO) and the separate MF application. The specific challenge is an error indicating difficulties with the eager consumption of a shared module. The error message I encounter is: `Uncaught Error: Shared module is not available for eager consumption: webpack/sharing/consume/default/react/react` ### Troubleshooting Efforts - I have extensively searched through many discussions, forums, and Webpack's official documentation, trying out various suggested solutions, but none have resolved the issue. - I've carefully configured the shared dependencies in both the CRA and MF applications. - I've confirmed the successful loading and response of remoteEntry.js from the MF application. - I've experimented with various configurations, including removing the eager flag, in an attempt to solve this issue. ### Environment **Container app** - React version: 18.2.0 - CRA version: 5.0.1 - CRACO version: 7.1.0 **MF app** - React version: 18.2.0 - Webpack version: 5.89.0 ### Project Structure and Code Samples **Container app** _craco.config.js_ _App.jsx_ **MF app** _structure_ _webpack.config.js_ _index.js_ _bootstrap.js_ _App.jsx_ Also I would like to share remoteEntry.js response

title: Webpack 5 is tree shaking all modules required using require.resolve
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

Webpack marks all the modules as  **unused harmony export** which gets tree shaken on the prod bundle

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

Most of the code is taken from this repo for reproduction - https://github.com/bradtraversy/webpack-starter/tree/main/src

**Changes Made**

- Added a **testMethod.js** file 
```
export default function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

```

- Added a **registry.js** file with following content
```
export default {
  testMethodModule: require.resolve("./testMethod"),
};
```
- Added following code to **generateJoke.js**

```
import registry from "./registry";
  
...
const testMethod = __webpack_require__(registry.testMethodModule);
  console.log(testMethod);
```

- Modified webpack.config.js to the following 

```
mode: "production",
  entry: {
    bundle: path.resolve(__dirname, "src/index.js"),
  },
  output: {
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist"),
    filename: "[name][contenthash].js",
    clean: true,
    assetModuleFilename: "[name][ext]",
  },
  devtool: "source-map",
  devServer: {
    static: {
      directory: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist"),
    },
    port: 4000,
    open: true,
    hot: true,
    compress: true,
    historyApiFallback: true,
  },
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.js$/,
        exclude: /node_modules/,
        use: {
          loader: "babel-loader",
          options: {
            presets: ["@babel/preset-env"],
          },
        },
      },
    ],
  },
  plugins: [
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      title: "Webpack App",
      filename: "index.html",
      template: "src/template.html",
    }),
    new BundleAnalyzerPlugin(),
  ],
  optimization: {
    minimize: false,
  },
```

When I run webpack dev server `NODE_ENV=production webpack serve`, following output is generated in bundle.js

```

/***/ 735:
/***/ ((__unused_webpack_module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) => {

"use strict";
/* unused harmony export default */
function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

/***/ }),
```

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

testMethod should not be marked as unused. This has broken in webpack 5 since the same app with webpack 4 produces the below output.

```
/***/ "./src/testMethod.js":
/*!***************************!*\
  !*** ./src/testMethod.js ***!
  \***************************/
/*! exports provided: default */
/***/ (function(module, __webpack_exports__, __webpack_require__) {

"use strict";
__webpack_require__.r(__webpack_exports__);
/* harmony export (binding) */ __webpack_require__.d(__webpack_exports__, "default", function() { return testMethod; });
function testMethod() {
  console.log("test method called");
}

/***/ }),

```

We rely heavily on this feature to register modules in our library.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.88.2
Node.js version: 20.8.0
Operating System: Mac OS ventura 13.6.2
Additional tools:

title: Hi, want to ask is it right to apply mergeDuplicateChunks after removeAvailableModules
body:
Hello, sorry to bother, currently in webpack, if we have a graph like this, the blue line represent dyn import(), the black line represents harmony import, the A, B, C, shared, m represent modules.

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/57202839/514e7cbb-9cbf-4676-9016-4602103bedd5)

We know after build chunkGraph, there are 2 chunks both contains shared, one is from B chunk, and one is from C chunk. And then code splitting can remove available modules, B chunk contains module `m`, shared chunk also contains `m`, so the shared chunk from B chunk can remove m module safely, while shared chunk from C chunk remains unchanged, this will cause the 2 chunk not the same, mergeDuplicateChunks won't merge those 2 chunks.

I know this design can reduce loaded chunk size in some scenes, but also in some cases this will increase total loaded chunk size, is this by design ?

Also I found that removeParentModulesPlugin and removeAvailableModules very similar, what's the differences between those 2

title: What is the decision-making process for choosing between the remote module and the host module when module is shared?
body:
Here is the reproducible repo (`node version >= 16`) 👉 https://github.com/tachish/ModuleFederationSharedDiscussion

**Boost Remote Project:**
```
cd ./host
npm i
npm run start
```
**Boost Host Project:**
```
cd ./remote
npm i
npm run start
```

Host Project: `http://localhost:3000/`
Remote Project: `http://localhost:3001/`

### Background:
Host project shares `vue` and `lodash` with remote project, only using configuration `singleton: true`. Versions of `vue` and `lodash` are literally same in host and remote.

I notice that host project loads remote project's `vue` instead of loading itself's, as following:
![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/37827090/ac57cdba-fb00-45bd-bf05-765333933754)
using `vue` from remote project (host: `http://localhost:3001`)

### Question:
I'm curious about the decision-making process between using the host module or the remote module. I'm unsure if this is determined by semver, or if the module loading sequence is initiated by the host project or the remote project.

title: splitChunks maxSize/minSize/maxRequest priority
body:
As stated on the website
[maxSize takes higher priority than maxInitialRequest/maxAsyncRequests. Actual priority is maxInitialRequest/maxAsyncRequests < maxSize < minSize.](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunksmaxsize)

After reading the source code, I found that the actual priority should be minSize>maxRequest>maxSize. 
- minSize check https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/0781eac69cf26af59b51513b3da9569f653ec64d/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L1309
- maxRequests check 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/0781eac69cf26af59b51513b3da9569f653ec64d/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L1419

- maxSize check
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/0781eac69cf26af59b51513b3da9569f653ec64d/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L1642

Not sure if I understood it wrong or the tips on the official website is wrong.

title: Entry dependOn does the opposite of what I expect
body:
I'm told the way to avoid duplicate code with multiple entries is to use the `dependOn` option in the entry definition. [Per the docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#dependencies):

> By default, every entry chunk stores all the modules that it uses. With `dependOn` option you can share the modules from one entry chunk to another:
> ```js
> module.exports = {
>  //...
>  entry: {
>    app: { import: './app.js', dependOn: 'react-vendors' },
>    'react-vendors': ['react', 'react-dom', 'prop-types'],
>  },
> };
>```
> The app chunk will not contain the modules that react-vendors have.

So an entry won't contain the code from another entry listed in `dependOn` - that part makes sense and is what happens.

Here is what I expect to happen:  
- I get two files as output, one for each entry.
- The `app` entry may import from `react-vendors`, but the code in `react-vendors` will not be included in the `app` bundle.
- I should include the `react-vendors` script on my page first. This should load all the code not included, but required, by `app`. Since `react-vendors` is just a dependency, I don't expect anything to happen except loading the module.
- When I include `app`, it should run and have access to the code previously loaded by `react-vendors`.

My issue lies with the last one or two points: the `app` entry does not produce a self-executing function - it produces some code that appears to be adding the module to a dependency array. Meanwhile, `react-vendors` *is* a self-executing function that is **not** added to the dependency array. 

```javascript
// app.js
(self["webpackChunkwebpack_etc"] = self["webpackChunkwebpack_etc"] || []).push([["app"],...])

// react-vendors.js
(() => { 'use-strict' ... })();
```

I would expect the exact opposite - `react-vendors` should be pushed to a dependency graph while `app` should just execute. 

Either this feature does not work as it is supposed to work, or it is poorly explained and never touches the subject of how this code is supposed to run.

[Example](https://github.com/ConnorJamesLow/webpack-dependOn-mre/tree/main)
author: ConnorJamesLow

title: Is it possible to do manual rebuilds with changed files (Node API)?
body:
Hi,

I've got a slightly complicated setup with multiple projects and packages which means that I need to be in full control of the order of builds and rebuilds. That means that I cannot use the Webpack file watcher (`compiler.watch`).

The `compiler`'s `run` method can be called multiple times though. 
If you are using the cache (memory) option, then trigger a second build sometime much later on, the build will run however no changes to files will be picked up (uses the cache as expected).

In my situation, fortunately I'm fully aware of what files have changed.
Is there a simple way to run a build, then invalidate specific cached files, then trigger another build?

Essentially what I'm looking for is to be able to:
- Call `compiler.run`
- Wait for build to finish
- *Sometime later a file changes*
- Do my processing
- Tell the `compiler` reference to invalidate its cache for the changed file(s)
- Call `compiler.run` again (uses cache for unchanged files)

Disabling the cache is an option, but would lose out quick build times.
I have noticed that the `compiler` has a `cache` property and `getCache` method, however there seems to be very little information / documentation about how to use them or if invalidating specific files is possible.

**TLDR:** Need to be able to do incremental rebuilds but without using the Webpack file watcher.

title: Exported object names not minified since 5.79

Since I updated to webpack 5.79+ in my project, bundle sizes have increased because some exported object names are not minified.

It turns out that this is due to those objects being destructured in modules where they are imported. 
Refactoring the code to remove the object destructuring minimizes the name again. 

Prior to 5.79, the names were minified regardless of the use of destructuring. 

Is this behavior aligned with #16941?

If this is not expected behavior, I'll share my configs and minimum reproducible code.

Looking forward to your thoughts and suggestions.

The latest version where the issue is reproducible is 5.90.

title: Eager consumption of a shared module if there are several versions of the module
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

First, some context to picture the problem.

I have a legacy project and I'm trying to use ModuleFederaionPlugin for step-by-step code replacement. I need to upgrade from vue v2 to vue3 in a very large codebase, so the ability to create isolated contexts and share common dependencies looks great to me. But there is still trouble. I have componential libraries, with some framework-independent logic (which I'd like to share), and two libraries based on them, dedicated to different versions of vue. Those libraries are under active development so I constantly receive updates, and my vue3 build is isolated in a nested workspace, and shared libs in that workspace often have newer versions than the libs in the root during development. Let the version in root be `0.24.23`, and `0.24.24` in the nested workspace.

Here are the options for a module federation in the root:
```javascript
const { dependencies } = require('./package.json')
const options = {
    name: 'host',
    remoteType: 'var',
    remotes: {
        buildNext: 'buildNext',
    },
    shared: {
        ...Object.keys(dependencies).reduce((shared, pkgName) => {
            if (/(some-module-i-do-not-want-to-share)/.test(pkgName)) {
                return shared
            }

            return {
                ...shared,
                [pkgName]: {
                    eager: true,
                    singleton: true,
                    requiredVersion: dependencies[pkgName],
                },
            }
        }, {}),
    },
}
```

For build-next:
```javascript
const { dependencies } = require('./package.json')
const options = {
    name: 'index',
    filename: ifEnvProduction()
        ? '[name].[contenthash:8].js'
        : '[name].[fullhash].js',
    library: { type: 'var', name: 'buildNext' },
    exposes: {
        './i18n': {
            name: 'i18n',
            import: './src/setLocale.ts',
        },
    },
    shared: {
        ...dependencies,
    },
}
```
So, if I restrict the version of my library in buildNext to `=0.24.23`, eager consumption works. But if I remove the restriction, the build process does not fail, but the code of my website doesn't work and I get the message `Shared module is not available for eager consumption`. I studied the problem and found out, that the loader doesn't take into account the eager flag of a shared entry.
I have a list of versions like
```
{
  "0.24.23": { eager: true, /* some other stuff */ },
  "0.24.24": { eager: false, /* some other stuff */ },
}
```
So the loader uses the method `findVersion` and moves to 0.24.24, which is not intended for eager consumption, so I get a promise instead of a function and then get the error message.

I also noticed, that if I have remote entries, my shared scope is flagged as a promise and according to loader logic, I should have gotten the error regardless of the eager flag, since I don't use `bootstrap` for the host. But there were no errors.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Some steps are above in the description.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
Eager consumption uses the correct version of my dependency. Well, actually, I've solved the problem locally by creating my own `ConsumeSharedModule` and `ConsumeSharedRuntimeModule`, and I need help with PR & review. I've read the `CONTRIBUTING.md` and its `Submitting Changes` section. I have trouble with the autotest step, not sure where I need to place them and where I can find existing tests for the plugin 😅 (to use them as an example, this is my first time testing things like this 😅).

Here is the commit with my changes for review: https://github.com/cmath10/webpack/commit/d5926d090cae6622eb8fa71c297cd3a2723c535c

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**

Node.js version: v16.20.2 (docker) / v18.17.1 (without docker)
Operating System: Linux Mint 21.3
Additional tools: ~

Looking at the `outgoingConnections` for a particular, I noticed that there were no dependencies present for any of the dynamic `import`statements.  The dependencies from the static `import` statements are there, but not the dynamic ones.

Is this expected that `incomingConnections` contains dependencies that are not present in `outgoingConnections` ?  I was expecting to be able to get the same dependency info regardless of which structure I look at.  But that doesn't seem to be the case.  Is it correct?

title: Empty file emitted for `await import('crypto')` in a dependency despite resolve.fallback.crypto = false
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

After upgrading a package---in this case, `bson` from 4.x to 5.x/6.x---webpack began emitting an additional chunk, named `_5ab2.js` in my case. And the URL used to try and fetch this file from the server doesn't seem to properly respect my `output.publicPath` config. The `publicPath` prefix is prepended twice.

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

After finding the reference to this file in the main entry point's generated code, I traced it back to a function in the bson package:

```javascript
const nodejsRandomBytes = await (async () => {
    try {
        return (await import('crypto')).randomBytes;
    }
    catch {
        return nodejsMathRandomBytes;
    }
})();
```

My webpack.config.js has had `resolve: { fallback: { crypto: false } }` for extensions `[".ts", ".tsx", ".js", ".jsx", ".mjs", ".cjs"]`, so that I don't believe it should be trying to import anything. Moreover, the emitted file is essentially empty:

```javascript
(self["webpackChunkscserver"] = self["webpackChunkscserver"] || []).push([["_5ab2"],{

/***/ "?5ab2":
/*!************************!*\
  !*** crypto (ignored) ***!
  \************************/
/***/ (() => {

/* (ignored) */

/***/ })

}]);
//# sourceMappingURL=_5ab2.js.map
```

When trying to load this file, the server gets a request for `/js/js/_5ab2.js`, to which it responds with a 404.

Relevant Config:
```javascript
  const frontendConfig = {
    experiments: { topLevelAwait: true },
    devtool: "source-map",
    resolve: {
      extensions: [".ts", ".tsx", ".js", ".jsx", ".mjs", ".cjs"],
      fallback: {
        crypto: false
      }
    },
    entry: {
      app: "./src/frontend/ts/main.tsx"
    },
    output: {
      path: path.join(__dirname, "public/js"),
      publicPath: "js/",
      filename: "[name].js",
      chunkFilename: "[name].js"
    },
    module: {
      rules: [{
        test: /src\/.*svg$/,
        use: [ "raw-loader" ]
      },
      ...
      ,{
        test: /\.tsx?$/,
        exclude: /node_modules/,
        use: [{
          loader: "ts-loader",
          options: {configFile: path.resolve(__dirname, "src", "frontend", "tsconfig.json")}
        }]
      }, {
        test: /\.wasm$/,
        type: "asset/resource"
      }]
    },
    externals: {
      "react": "React",
      "react-dom": "ReactDOM"
    },
    plugins: [
      new webpack.DefinePlugin({
        secrets: ...
      }),
      new MiniCssExtractPlugin({
        filename: "extracted_styles.css"
      })
    ]
  };
```

my package.json has `"type": "module"`.

I have tried `require.resolve('crypto-browserify')` in the config, too, but the URL is still erroneous (and the output file is just a lot more comments... due to tree shaking, I think).

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Ideally, no extra file would be emitted since it's unnecessary. MongoDB's documentation for `bson` and the presence of the try/catch block all suggest an intention that the import should fail in the browser. 

If the empty file must be emitted, though, the URL should be correct when the system tries to fetch it.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.90.0
Node.js version: 18.19.0
Operating System: macOS 14.2.1
Additional tools: webpack-cli@5.1.4

title: Use of chunkReason in webpack SplitChunksPlugin
body:
As of now, Im thinking to use `WebpackManifestPlugin` to generate json file, which I would use to determine the js files to prefetch before even actually using the application.

This works good but in this json file, I would like to avoid the files that are lazily imported in application(via `import()`). As these files are anyway lazily imported, I might not need them on demand and hence can be delayed or avoided to be prefetched.
I was going through the chunks and found that each and every chunk has some field called `chunkReason`. Majorly I saw that `chunkReason` seems to be `split chunk (cache group: defaultVendors)` [in case of lazily imported chunks](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/SplitChunksPlugin.js#L1524).
Can i fairly assume that I can use `chunkReason` as an identifier to separate lazy loaded chunks? Or is it possible to have any identifier from `Chunk` to let me know if it is lazily loaded?

title: How does one dynamically import optional dependencies?
body:
I cannot either figure it out from the docs or from other sources online the answer to this question:

**How can I dynamically import a package (eg. `import('abc')`) which is an optional peer dependency?**

I am building a library which has optional features that dynamically import the optional peer dependencies. The app consuming my library tries to build with webpack. But this will result in the build failing with `ModuleNotFoundError` if the optional peer dependency isn't there. But since the dependency is optional (defined in `package.json`) this is wrong. If I instruct webpack to ignore the import with a magic comment then the dependency isn't bundled in case it's there, so it will always be missing. I just can't find the middle ground where I can just:

```js
import('abc')
  .then(/* the peer dep is there */)
  .catch(/* do something in case the optional peer dep isn't there*/)
```

This seems like an obvious use-case to me, since package managers allow one to express the requirement of an *optional* peer dependency. Am I having a wrong expectation?

_I know that some libraries take the approach of exposing a different entry point for the optional feature but that's besides the point, because that doesn't relate to dynamic imports._

_I also know that this works if one wants to *require* the package in a try...catch, but that's also besides the point. (and not feasible in my case anyway)_

title: How to choose the value of the option `splitChunks.chunks`?
body:
As title. From the documentation, quoted from [there](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunkschunks):

> This indicates which chunks will be selected for optimization. When a string is provided, valid values are `all`, `async`, and `initial`. **Providing `all` can be particularly powerful**, because it means that chunks can be shared even between async and non-async chunks.

Without the knowledge of the implementation details of webpack, I will always choose `all` since it says it's _particularly powerful_. What are the considerations I should be aware of before adopting this always-`all`-strategy? (I did try to read the source but I still don't know the drawbacks of always choosing `all`)

title: Why `optimization.removeAvailableModules` is default to `false`?
body:
According to the current [main](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/bca4ee1cc24dfa227ae222a78324237fa4d219d7/lib/config/defaults.js#L1342) its default value is `false`. Also, the current [documentation](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationremoveavailablemodules) seems outdated because it says:

> [...] Enabled by default in [production mode](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/).

Why is it not enabled by default in production mode? Is it because that SplitChunksPlugin has covered its job? There is a warning that provides a clue why it's set to `false` now:

> `optimization.removeAvailableModules` reduces the performance of webpack, and **will be disabled in production mode by default in next major release**. Disable it in production mode if you want extra build performance.

But it doesn't provide any reason or link to any related issue.

title: webpack-dev-server v5 seems to require `'unsafe-eval'` in CSP
body: I'm opening this as a discussion first to get input as this could either be a bug or documentation issue with `webpack-dev-server`. In a nutshell I've updated a Rails application generated using [this template](https://github.com/ackama/rails-template) which defines a [CSP](https://github.com/ackama/rails-template/blob/main/variants/backend-base/config/initializers/content_security_policy.rb) and includes some [extras for `webpack-dev-server`](https://github.com/ackama/rails-template/blob/main/variants/backend-base/config/initializers/content_security_policy.rb#L94); after upgrading to v5 however, I'm now getting an error about eval'd JS: Apparently this is happening here: Which is from node_modules/webpack-dev-server/client/modules/logger/index.js. --- I've had a look around and can't find anything indicating what has actually changed in v5 to cause this (my assumption is maybe a different build config, but could be something else, and this might just be the first of a number of these calls 🤷), but ideally it would be good if we could somehow avoid having to change the CSP since we want it to match as closely to production as possible for debugging and in particular enabling `'unsafe-eval'` is a big one (I have tried looking into using a nonce here, but couldn't get webpack to do so?) I'm happy to help out with this, but need some pointers from the webpack team to continue :)

title: Deduping ESM and CJS versions of the same package
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

When part of the codebase `import 'some-dep'` and transient dependencies `require('some-dep')`, and `some-dep`'s `package.json` looks like this:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "browser": {
        "require": "./dist/browser-cjs/index.js",
        "import": "./dist/esm2017/index.js"
      },
      "default": "./dist/esm2017/index.js"
  }
}
```

As it's the case with most `@firebase/*` packages - this results in both `dist/browser-cjs/index.js` and `dist/esm2017/index.js` to be included separately.

In this situation, it would be nice to have a way to force using the same dist file to minimize bundle size.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

Minimizing bundle size.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Not sure, and not even sure if it's fixable, I just want to start the conversation about this issue
author: valeriangalliat

title: css-loader: className not directly accessible from the default import when `css-loader` is the only loader and `namedExport: false`
body:
As title. Given that `namedExport: false`,

```css
.someClass { ... }
```

```js
import styles from './test.css'

console.log(styles.locals.someClass) // this will return `className`.
console.log(styles.someClass)        // this will return `undefined`.
```

but when `namedExport: true`,

```js
import styles from './test.css'

// cannot access `className` from the default export at all.
console.log(styles.locals)    // this is undefined.
console.log(styles.someClass) // this is undefined.
```
```js
import * as styles from './test.css'

console.log(styles.someClass) // this will return the `className`.
```

Why are those `className`s not directly accessible from the default export in both cases? But from the comment linked below, it seems that those `className`s are accessible if `style-loader` is also used after `css-loader`. Why this design?


ref: https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/issues/692#issuecomment-457918645

I want to bundle all css files to one file for example `index.css`

According to docs:
https://webpack.js.org/plugins/mini-css-extract-plugin/#extracting-all-css-in-a-single-file

I use `optimization.splitChunks.cacheGroups` and this works fine

But when i add `ModuleFederationPlugin` i have error:

```
ERROR in SplitChunksPlugin
Cache group "styles" conflicts with existing chunk.
Both have the same name "index" and existing chunk is not a parent of the selected modules.
Use a different name for the cache group or make sure that the existing chunk is a parent (e. g. via dependOn).
HINT: You can omit "name" to automatically create a name.
BREAKING CHANGE: webpack < 5 used to allow to use an entrypoint as splitChunk. This is no longer allowed when the entrypoint is not a parent of the selected modules.
Remove this entrypoint and add modules to cache group's 'test' instead. If you need modules to be evaluated on startup, add them to the existing entrypoints (make them arrays). See migration guide of more info.

webpack 5.90.1 compiled with 1 error in 36 ms
```

**Here is an example repo with reproduction:**
https://github.com/krutoo/webpack-mf-css-modules-bundle

Is it possible to generate one file with all css with same name as entry point without modify source code of my project?

I want this file to be used both in the remote entry point and in the main entry point

#### Details

Based on previously not a very correct question https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/18074
author: krutoo

author: yoghurtxu

title: Understanding about webpack loaders
body:
I made a file format "ABC" which I can transpile to Typescript.

Next, I want to load the transpiled Typescript every time I reference the `.abc` file. Until now I thought this can be done with a custom webpack loader. I saw this working for CSS, where the loader adds simple JSON objects with names of all CSS classes.

```ts
import { $UserType } from "./Blog.abc";

const user: $UserType = {
  $type: "UserType",
  id: 1,
  name: 'John Doe',
  email: '',
  createdAt: '',
  updatedAt: '',
};

console.log(JSON.stringify(user, null, 2));
```

But my loader does not work. So I cloned the CSS loader and just replaced the CSS regexp with my ABC  regexp. But nothing happens.

The loader is imported by the following webpack config line. But a profiling told me that it was not used by  webpack...

```js
{ test: /\.abc$/, use: ["ts-loader", "@abc/webpack-loader"]  },
```

My whole setup is in a monorepo.

Is my understanding of loaders correct?
What am I missing?
author: Lotes

title: Could you explain the usage of webpack-dev-middleware?
body:
Hi! I'm learning webpack and I just learned that `webpack-dev-server` internally uses `webpack-dev-middleware`. My question is: could `webpack-dev-middleware` be used with any backend server framework other than express.js?

From its [README](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-middleware?tab=readme-ov-file#webpack-dev-middleware) they say:

> An **express-style** development middleware for use with [webpack](https://webpack.js.org/) bundles and allows for serving of the files emitted from webpack. This should be used for development only.
author: nyngwang

title: Webpack upgrade from v4 to v5 loader errors
body:
Hello folks, I have a project that was recently upgraded from webpack v4 to webpack v5. I followed the migration guide here https://webpack.js.org/migrate/5/ and everything so far has been going swell with the exception of encountering a few errors related to tests files which should be excluded causing issues when building the application. 


Error: 

```js
Module parse failed: Unexpected token (13:33)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
| 
|   test('should render basic HTML', () => {
>     const { container } = render(<SessionExpiredPage {...mockBasicProps} />);
|     expect(container).toMatchSnapshot();
|   });
 @ ./src/pages/ lazy ^\.\/.*$ namespace object ./session-expired-page/session-expired-page.presentation.test.jsx ./session-expired-page/session-expired-page.presentation.test
 @ ./src/core/router/routes.js 5:11-44
 @ ./src/core/router/Router.jsx 1:335-364 1:819-835 1:902-921 1:998-1023 1:1271-1287 1:1362-1379 1:1457-1477 1:1575-1591 1:1694-1718 1:1815-1830 1:1929-1944 1:2048-2073 1:2172-2193 1:2294-2315 1:2412-2438 1:2594-2622 1:2725-2749 1:2854-2880 1:2992-3025 1:3122-3141 1:3237-3255 1:3358-3375 1:3477-3501 1:3594-3609 1:3708-3728 1:3826-3846 1:3960-3980 1:4076-4094 1:4216-4242 1:4368-4397 1:4521-4548 1:4646-4663 1:4756-4777 1:4864-4890 1:4966-4990 1:5028-5047
 @ ./src/core/router/index.js 1:0-40 1:0-40
 @ ./src/index.jsx 1:199-231 1:1092-1098
 @ ./src/index.js 1:320-349 1:1279-1282

webpack 5.90.0 compiled with 9 errors in 62126 ms
```

I've combed through documentation to exclude `.test.js` and `*.test.jsx` files however it seems they are still being included. 
I've also added the exclusions to my babel config. I'm not sure at this point how to proceed. Has anyone encountered a similar issue?

As an aside, we have another team that is working on a similar project and we have pretty similar packages and webpack configs but they are not encountering the issue. 

Any guidance in the right direction would be greatly appreciated.
author: abrahamcuenca
